items.drill: Cascading Timeout Failure
items.scenario: Service A calls B calls C. Service C starts responding in 5s instead of 200ms. B's thread pool fills up. A starts returning 503s. Users see full outage despite A and B being healthy.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Write a 3-service mock (Python/Go) where C injects latency
2. Diagnose: Use distributed traces to pinpoint C as root cause vs misleading B errors
3. Fix: Implement circuit breaker on B→C with fallback response
4. Validate: Verify A stays healthy when C degrades
5. Prevent: Add timeout budgets and per-hop deadlines

items.drill: Memory Leak in Long-Running Service
items.scenario: API service memory grows 50MB/hour. No OOM yet but approaching container limit. Restarts buy time but leak returns. Heap dumps show growing HashMap of user sessions that aren't being evicted.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Write a Flask/Express app with an in-memory cache that never evicts
2. Diagnose: Use pprof (Go) or memory_profiler (Python) to identify the leak source
3. Hotfix: Add TTL-based eviction, deploy without downtime
4. Validate: Monitor RSS over 30 min to confirm flat memory curve
5. Root fix: Replace in-memory cache with Redis, add max-size bounds

items.drill: Poison Pill Request
items.scenario: One user's request contains a 50MB JSON body. It passes validation but causes the handler to OOM. The request retries infinitely from a queue, crashing workers in a loop.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Send oversized payload to a mock API, observe worker crash + retry loop
2. Diagnose: Correlate crash timestamps with specific request IDs in logs
3. Hotfix: Add request body size limit at API gateway, dead-letter the poison message
4. Validate: Confirm normal requests flow, poison pill is quarantined
5. Prevent: Add payload size limits, circuit breaker on repeated failures from same message

items.drill: Rate Limit Bypass
items.scenario: A bot is hitting your API at 10K rps from rotating IPs. Per-IP rate limiting is ineffective. The bot is scraping user profiles and your database CPU is at 95%.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Script a multi-IP load generator against a mock API with per-IP rate limits
2. Diagnose: Identify the pattern — same user-agent, sequential profile IDs, no auth tokens
3. Hotfix: Add compound rate limiting (IP + user-agent + endpoint), temporary block on /profiles
4. Validate: Legitimate users unaffected, bot traffic drops
5. Harden: Add fingerprinting, CAPTCHA challenges, read replicas for scrape-heavy endpoints

items.drill: Slow Query Bringing Down the Database
items.scenario: A new feature shipped a query with a missing index. It does a full table scan on a 500M row table. DB CPU pegs at 100%, all other queries queue behind it. Dashboard shows P99 latency spike from 50ms to 12s.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Create a 1M row table, run a SELECT without index, observe EXPLAIN ANALYZE
2. Diagnose: Use pg_stat_activity / SHOW PROCESSLIST to find the offending query
3. Hotfix: Kill long-running queries, add index with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (no downtime)
4. Validate: Confirm P99 drops back, no lock contention during index build
5. Prevent: Add query plan review to CI, slow query log alerting

items.drill: Disk Full on Primary Database
items.scenario: Primary Postgres disk hits 95%. WAL segments are piling up because a replication slot for a dead replica is holding them. Writes will fail at 100%.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Set up Postgres with replication, kill replica, watch WAL accumulate
2. Diagnose: Check pg_replication_slots, pg_wal_lsn_diff, disk usage breakdown
3. Hotfix: Drop the dead replication slot to release WAL, verify disk drops
4. Validate: Primary writes resume, remaining replicas unaffected
5. Prevent: Alert on replication slot lag, auto-drop slots with >X GB lag

items.drill: Cache Stampede / Thundering Herd
items.scenario: A popular cache key expires. 10K requests simultaneously miss cache and hit the database. DB connection pool exhausts, cascading failure across all endpoints.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Set a hot key in Redis with short TTL, simulate concurrent requests on expiry
2. Diagnose: Monitor cache hit/miss ratio, DB connection pool exhaustion, request queue depth
3. Hotfix: Implement cache lock (singleflight) — only one request rebuilds, others wait
4. Validate: On expiry, exactly 1 DB query fires, others serve from refreshed cache
5. Harden: Add jittered TTLs, background refresh before expiry, stale-while-revalidate

items.drill: Data Corruption from Concurrent Writes
items.scenario: Two API servers process the same order simultaneously. Both read balance=$100, both deduct $80, both write balance=$20. Customer was charged $160 but balance shows $20 instead of -$60. Money is lost.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Simulate two concurrent read-modify-write cycles on same row without locking
2. Diagnose: Identify the lost update — both reads see stale state, last write wins
3. Hotfix: Add SELECT FOR UPDATE or optimistic locking with version column
4. Validate: Concurrent deductions correctly fail or serialize
5. Design: Implement idempotency keys for payment operations

items.drill: PostgreSQL Transaction ID Wraparound — Emergency Vacuum Under Active Blocker
items.scenario:
At 03:22 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'postgres-prod-ledger txid_age_critical 2,024,380,121 XIDs (threshold: 1,500,000,000)'. Your PostgreSQL 15.4 cluster (AWS RDS db.r6g.16xlarge, Multi-AZ, us-east-1) backs a fintech payment ledger. Postgres server logs via CloudWatch:

  2026-04-22T03:19:41 UTC [31272] WARNING:  database "ledger_prod" must be vacuumed within 123,103,527 transactions
  2026-04-22T03:19:41 UTC [31272] HINT:  To avoid a database shutdown, execute a database-wide VACUUM in "ledger_prod".

SELECT relname, age(relfrozenxid) FROM pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r' ORDER BY age DESC LIMIT 5 returns:

  transactions   | 2,024,380,121
  audit_events   | 1,998,204,847
  account_ledger | 1,899,003,210

The `transactions` table (1.1B rows, 1.8TB) has not been successfully auto-vacuumed in 11.2 hours. pg_stat_activity shows:
  pid=28844, state='idle in transaction', query_start='2026-04-21 15:47:33 UTC' (11.6h ago), wait_event=NULL, query='SELECT account_id, SUM(amount) FROM transactions WHERE created_at BETWEEN ''2025-01-01'' AND ''2026-01-01'' GROUP BY account_id'
  pid=29033, state='active', query='autovacuum: VACUUM ANALYZE ledger_prod.transactions', wait_event_type='Lock', wait_event='relation', blocked_by=28844

The analytics ETL job (pid=28844) holds an `AccessShareLock` on the `transactions` table. `idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 0` (disabled on this RDS instance — a misconfiguration). autovacuum worker pid=29033 has been blocked for 4.7 hours. XID consumption rate from high write load: ~52,000 XIDs/second. Remaining XIDs before hard wraparound (2,147,483,648): 123,103,527. Time to wraparound: ~39 minutes. If wraparound occurs, all writes fail: ERROR: database is not accepting commands to avoid wraparound data loss in database "ledger_prod". Business impact: payment processing completely offline, $2.8M/min revenue halt.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose: Confirm age severity — SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) FROM pg_database ORDER BY age DESC. Identify the blocker chain via pg_blocking_pids(29033). Check if additional autovacuum workers are queued: SELECT * FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 0 OR last_autovacuum IS NULL ORDER BY age(relfrozenxid) DESC. Calculate exact time-to-wraparound: (2147483648 - age(relfrozenxid)) / (current_xid_rate_per_sec).
2. Immediate mitigation: Terminate the blocking analytics session — SELECT pg_terminate_backend(28844). Verify the autovacuum worker unblocks within 5s by re-checking pg_stat_activity. Do NOT kill with pg_cancel_backend (that only cancels the query but leaves the transaction open — the lock remains). pg_terminate_backend closes the connection entirely.
3. Emergency VACUUM: If autovacuum does not self-start within 60s (it may be sleeping between cycles), trigger manually as a superuser: VACUUM FREEZE ANALYZE VERBOSE ledger_prod.transactions. Monitor progress in real-time via pg_stat_progress_vacuum — check blocks_done vs blocks_total. On a 1.8TB table this can take 30–60 min; track relfrozenxid age dropping in pg_class during the run.
4. Buy time if VACUUM is too slow: If wraparound is <10 min away and VACUUM hasn't started, immediately enable autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay = 0 (no throttle) via ALTER SYSTEM SET autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay = 0; SELECT pg_reload_conf(); to make autovacuum run at full I/O speed. Also: SET maintenance_work_mem = '8GB' for the manual VACUUM session to speed up freeze processing.
5. Validate: After VACUUM completes, confirm SELECT age(relfrozenxid) FROM pg_class WHERE relname = 'transactions' drops to near 0 (< 10M). Check pg_database age drops below autovacuum_freeze_max_age (200M default). Verify no write failures in application error logs.
6. Prevent: (a) Set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '3600000' (1 hour) on the RDS parameter group — long-running analytics jobs must use read replicas, not the primary. (b) Route all analytics queries to a read replica via PgBouncer. (c) Add a Datadog monitor on custom metric: SELECT age(datfrozenxid) FROM pg_database WHERE datname = current_database() — alert at 500M (warning) and 1B (critical). (d) Add autovacuum_freeze_max_age = 150000000 (150M) to catch the issue earlier. (e) Ensure nightly autovacuum health check: SELECT relname, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE last_autovacuum < NOW() - INTERVAL '24h' AND n_live_tup > 10000000.

items.drill: MongoDB Atlas Replica Set — Oplog Window Exhausted by Unindexed Analytics Query Triggering Resync Cascade
items.scenario:
At 19:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'product-catalog-db-prod secondary-replication-lag > 3600s (threshold: 30s)'. Your MongoDB Atlas M80 cluster (3-node replica set, AWS us-east-1, 4.8TB total data, ~8,200 write ops/sec peak) backs a product catalog and personalized recommendation service. A data scientist's analytics script ran an unindexed aggregation directly against the secondary (using `readPreference: 'secondary'`):

  db.products.aggregate([
    {$unwind: "$variants"},
    {$unwind: "$tags"},
    {$group: {_id: "$tags", total_revenue: {$sum: "$variants.sold_price"}}},
    {$sort: {total_revenue: -1}}
  ], {allowDiskUse: true})

The query ran for 4h 37min (16:32–21:09 UTC) on secondary rs1.atlas-xm90-prod.mongodb.net. MongoDB 7.0's replication relies on a single replication thread that must apply oplog entries in order; the secondary's oplog applier thread was not directly blocked but *collection-level write-concern waits* from in-flight replicated writes caused secondary lag to grow as the collection scan held reader attention. The primary continued at 8,200 ops/sec, generating ~29.5 GB/hr in the oplog. MongoDB Atlas oplog window for this M80 cluster: 90 minutes (oplog capped at 267 GB; at 8,200 writes/sec × avg 1.1KB/op = 29.5 GB/hr, the window is only 9 hours — but Atlas had previously reduced it to 90 min during a disk-pressure event and never reverted). When the analytics query completed at 21:09 UTC, the secondary attempted to resume replication from its lastApplied timestamp 4h 37min ago. Replication error:

  2026-04-24T21:09:14.822+0000 I REPL [replication-0] Error fetching next batch of oplog entries:
  { code: 136, codeName: "CappedPositionLost",
    errmsg: "Resume token not found in the oplog.
    Resume timestamp: Timestamp(1745512332, 1) (2026-04-24T16:32:12Z).
    Oldest oplog timestamp: Timestamp(1745523914, 1) (2026-04-24T21:05:14Z).
    You have fallen off the back of the oplog." }
  2026-04-24T21:09:14.831+0000 W REPL [replication-0] Initial sync needed — oplog recovery is not possible.

Atlas initiates automatic initial sync. With 4.8TB of data, Atlas uses snapshot + oplog replay: resync completes in 88 minutes. However, 45 minutes into the resync (21:45 UTC), an Atlas-scheduled maintenance rolling restart begins on the *remaining* healthy secondary. For 6 minutes (21:51–21:57 UTC), the primary is the sole running node. All write operations with w:majority fail:

  MongoServerError: waiting for replication timed out
  { ok: 0, code: 50, codeName: 'MaxTimeMSExpired', errmsg: 'operation exceeded time limit',
    errLabels: ['RetryableWriteError'], writeConcernError: {code: 91, codeName: 'ShutdownInProgress'} }

340K product catalog writes fail. 2.1M recommendation API calls serve stale data from a 4h-old snapshot cached in Redis. Business impact: $280K estimated revenue impact during peak evening traffic window.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose: Check Atlas Performance Advisor and Replication Lag dashboard (Atlas UI → Metrics → Replication Lag). Confirm lag started at 16:32 UTC — correlate with operations log: db.currentOp({op: 'command', 'secs_running': {$gte: 600}}) on the secondary at the time (if you had a monitoring agent). Post-incident, use Atlas Logs: `mongocryptd` audit log shows 'aggregate' command issued at 16:32:07 UTC with planSummary: 'COLLSCAN'. Check oplog window: rs.printReplicationInfo() → log length: 90 minutes. Cross-reference primary oplog oldest timestamp with secondary's lastApplied to confirm secondary fell off.
2. Immediate mitigation for the w:majority outage: Application retry logic should handle code 91 (ShutdownInProgress) with a 30s retry window — confirm this is implemented. If not, add exponential backoff retry in the MongoDB driver client options: `retryWrites: true, serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 30000`. Manually check Atlas cluster status every 2 minutes: wait for the secondary under maintenance to rejoin (Atlas completes rolling restarts in 90s per node). w:majority writes resume once the restarted secondary is electable.
3. Root cause — two compounding failures: (a) Oplog window too short (90 min) for a cluster with 8,200 ops/sec write rate. This was set during a disk-pressure event and never reverted via Atlas API or UI. (b) Analytics queries running directly on the secondary with `allowDiskUse: true` — a COLLSCAN taking 4.6h causes oplog lag accumulation via reader pressure on the secondary's WiredTiger storage engine.
4. Stop recurrence: (a) Force-terminate any secondary query >30 min: db.adminCommand({currentOp: 1, op: 'command', secs_running: {$gte: 1800}}) → db.adminCommand({killOp: 1, op: <opid>}). Add a monitoring script (cronjob) that runs this check every 5 minutes. (b) Increase Atlas oplog window: In Atlas UI → Cluster Settings → Oplog Size → set to minimum 24 hours of oplog retention (at 29.5 GB/hr = 708 GB — select M80 disk tier with 4× oplog sizing). Atlas API: PATCH /api/atlas/v1.0/groups/{groupId}/clusters/{clusterName} {"replicationSpecs": [{"oplogSizeMB": 716800}]}. (c) Create a dedicated analytics read-only Atlas cluster (free using Atlas Online Archive or a separate M30 hidden secondary) — route all analytics scripts to it. Block `allowDiskUse: true` on the operational secondary via MongoDB user role restrictions.
5. Validate: After resync completes and maintenance window closes: rs.status() shows all 3 members with health:1 and stateStr:'PRIMARY'/'SECONDARY'. rs.printReplicationInfo() confirms oplog window is ≥ 24h. Run db.products.getPlanCache().clear() and re-run the analytics query on the dedicated analytics cluster — confirm it uses an index (add composite index: {tags: 1, 'variants.sold_price': 1}) or completes in <5 min.
6. Prevent: (a) Add Atlas alert on 'Replication Oplog Window < 4 hours' — fires before lag becomes critical. (b) Add Atlas alert on 'Replication Lag > 60s' — PagerDuty P2 at 60s, P1 at 300s (5 min). (c) Enforce index usage for analytics queries: set `$comment: 'analytics'` on all analytics queries and add a query audit rule that alerts when any query with this comment uses COLLSCAN. (d) Coordinate Atlas maintenance windows with the oplog resync status — add Atlas API check before maintenance: GET /api/atlas/v1.0/groups/{groupId}/clusters/{clusterName}/replicationSpec and verify all members are in sync before allowing rolling restart.

items.drill: Cassandra Tombstone Accumulation Kills Feature Store Reads
items.scenario: At 09:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'feature-store-service error rate 12.3% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Cassandra 4.1 cluster (12-node, AWS EC2 i4i.4xlarge, us-east-1) backs the user_preferences feature table consumed by three ML ranking models. Error logs flood with: `com.datastax.driver.core.exceptions.ReadTimeoutException: Cassandra timeout during read query at consistency LOCAL_QUORUM (2 responses were required but only 1 was obtained)`. The query `SELECT feature_vector FROM feature_store.user_preferences WHERE user_id=? ORDER BY updated_at DESC LIMIT 10` is scanning an average of 218,000 tombstones per partition to return 10 live rows — confirmed by `nodetool tablehistograms feature_store user_preferences` (p99 tombstones_scanned: 218432). The table has been live for 9 months and the application uses a DELETE-then-INSERT pattern on every user preference update (~42K writes/sec). `nodetool tpstats` shows ReadStage: pending=1,102, blocked=287. GC logs on coordinator nodes reveal back-to-back G1GC full pauses of 5.8s, 6.1s, 4.9s starting at 09:44 UTC. P99 read latency: 9ms → 6.8s. The three ML models are now serving stale cached features from 35 minutes ago.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose: Run `nodetool tablehistograms feature_store user_preferences` to confirm tombstone histogram (p50/p99/pMax). Cross-check with `nodetool cfstats feature_store.user_preferences | grep -iE 'tombstone|live'`. Check compaction strategy — likely SizeTieredCompactionStrategy (STCS) which defers compaction and lets tombstones accumulate
2. Hotfix (immediate): Reduce gc_grace_seconds from 864000 to 86400 on the table: `ALTER TABLE feature_store.user_preferences WITH gc_grace_seconds=86400;`. Then trigger rolling compaction node by node: `nodetool compact feature_store user_preferences` (monitor ReadStage clearing before proceeding to next node). This should clear 80–90% of tombstones within 20–30 min per node
3. Application fix: Replace DELETE+INSERT pattern with INSERT USING TTL — `INSERT INTO user_preferences (user_id, updated_at, feature_vector) VALUES (?, ?, ?) USING TTL 604800;` (7-day TTL). Rows now expire via SSTable TTL metadata — zero tombstone writes. Deploy this change before compaction completes on remaining nodes
4. Validate: After compaction on first 3 nodes, rerun `nodetool tablehistograms` — tombstones_scanned p99 should drop below 500. ReadStage pending clears. P99 read latency returns to ~12ms within 8 minutes of node 1 compaction completing
5. Long-term: Switch compaction strategy to TimeWindowCompactionStrategy (TWCS) which is purpose-built for time-series with TTLs and compacts within time buckets to avoid cross-bucket tombstone amplification. Add Datadog alert on `cassandra.db.tombstone_scanned_histogram.p99 > 5000` per table. Establish a monthly `nodetool tablehistograms` review as part of SRE capacity review

items.drill: MySQL InnoDB Gap Lock Deadlock Storm — Inventory Reservation Cascade During Flash Sale
items.layer: Database (MySQL 8.0.32 / AWS RDS for MySQL) / Application (Java Spring Boot / AWS EKS)
items.scenario:
At 11:00:02 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'inventory-service-prod error_rate 34.7% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Java Spring Boot inventory reservation service (16 pods, AWS EKS us-east-1, c6i.4xlarge) handles order placement for a flash sale across 500K SKUs. MySQL 8.0.32 RDS Multi-AZ (db.r6g.8xlarge, io2 2TB, us-east-1) processes reservations. Error logs flood:

  2026-05-12T11:00:03.281Z ERROR InventoryService - com.mysql.cj.jdbc.exceptions.MySQLTransactionRollbackException: Deadlock found when trying to get lock; try restarting transaction
  Error Code: 1213, SQL State: 40001
  Query: UPDATE inventory SET reserved_qty = reserved_qty + ? WHERE product_id = ? AND warehouse_id = ? AND available_qty >= ?
  [4,847 identical errors in the first 30 seconds]

SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS (accessed via RDS Enhanced Monitoring → MySQL error log) reveals:

  ------------------------
  LATEST DETECTED DEADLOCK
  ------------------------
  2026-05-12 11:00:02 UTC
  *** (1) TRANSACTION:
  TRANSACTION 8723441, ACTIVE 0 sec starting index read
  MySQL thread id 14882, query id 29483021 10.0.7.41 svc_inventory updating
  UPDATE inventory SET reserved_qty = reserved_qty + 1
  WHERE product_id = 'SKU-XF792' AND warehouse_id = 3 AND available_qty >= 1
  *** (1) HOLDS THE LOCK(S):
  RECORD LOCKS index idx_product_warehouse of table `inventory_db`.`inventory`
  lock_mode X locks gap before rec
  Record: product_id='SKU-XF792', warehouse_id=4
  *** (1) WAITING FOR THIS LOCK TO BE GRANTED:
  lock_mode X locks gap before rec insert intention waiting
  *** (2) TRANSACTION:
  TRANSACTION 8723438, ACTIVE 0 sec inserting
  MySQL thread id 14879, query id 29483018 10.0.7.39 svc_inventory
  INSERT INTO order_reservations (reservation_id, product_id, warehouse_id, order_id, qty, created_at)
  VALUES (UUID(), 'SKU-XF792', 3, ?, 1, NOW())
  *** (2) HOLDS THE LOCK(S):
  lock_mode X locks gap before rec insert intention
  *** (2) WAITING FOR THIS LOCK TO BE GRANTED:
  lock_mode X locks gap before rec WAITING
  *** WE ROLL BACK TRANSACTION (1)

RDS CloudWatch at 11:00–11:04 UTC: DatabaseConnections=1,847 (pool max=2,000), MySQL:Deadlocks=1,420/min (baseline: 0.3/min), DMLLatency P99=8.7s (baseline: 12ms), CPUUtilization=91%.

Root cause: The UPDATE uses WHERE available_qty >= 1 against composite index idx_product_warehouse(product_id, warehouse_id). Under MySQL 8.0 REPEATABLE READ isolation, when warehouse_id=3 is out of stock (0 available_qty), InnoDB acquires a gap lock covering index range (SKU-XF792, warehouse_id=2) → (SKU-XF792, warehouse_id=4) to prevent phantom reads. A concurrent INSERT INTO order_reservations — which shares the same index via a foreign key on (product_id, warehouse_id) — tries to acquire an Insert Intention Lock on that same gap. Two concurrent transactions each hold a gap lock on their respective SKU's range and block each other's Insert Intention Locks → deadlock. At 8,400 concurrent order attempts during flash sale launch (vs. baseline 140/sec), deadlock probability escalates from <0.01% to 34.7%. Spring @Retryable with zero-millisecond backoff amplifies load 3× on each 1213 error. Business impact: 34.7% of flash sale orders fail in the first 4 minutes; 22K customers see 'Item could not be reserved'; $490K estimated revenue impact.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm deadlocks — not just connection saturation — are root cause. Check CloudWatch metric MySQL:Deadlocks (1,420/min vs 0.3/min baseline). Connect to RDS via SSM Session Manager: SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS\G — find 'LATEST DETECTED DEADLOCK' section; identify transactions, index names, and lock types (gap lock vs insert intention lock). Run SELECT * FROM performance_schema.data_lock_waits\G for live lock waiters. Rule out metadata lock contention: SHOW FULL PROCESSLIST — filter for 'Waiting for table metadata lock' state. Confirm the gap lock root cause: the UPDATE on idx_product_warehouse with available_qty >= 1 predicate targets a non-existent row (out-of-stock), triggering gap lock acquisition.
2. Immediate mitigation (90 seconds): (a) Rate-limit concurrent DB calls — reduce HikariCP maximumPoolSize per pod from 50→15 via ConfigMap + kubectl rollout restart (1 pod at a time). With 16 pods × 15 connections = 240 max vs 1,847 current, connection pressure drops and reduces concurrent gap lock holders. (b) Fix retry amplification — change @Retryable(maxAttempts=3, backoff=@Backoff(delay=0)) to @Backoff(delay=200, multiplier=2, random=true), spreading retries 200ms–1.6s. Deploy via Spring Cloud Config dynamic refresh (no pod restart needed). These two changes should reduce error rate from 34.7% to <5% within 90s.
3. Fix the gap lock — switch transaction isolation for this path: READ COMMITTED does not acquire gap locks, only record locks. In Spring, annotate the reservation method: @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED). READ COMMITTED is safe here — there is no phantom-read requirement; you only need to see the current available_qty at the instant of write. This change eliminates gap lock acquisition entirely. Deploy via rolling restart.
4. Durable root fix — atomic point-lookup with SKIP LOCKED: Replace the range UPDATE with a two-step atomic pattern: (a) SELECT id FROM inventory WHERE product_id=? AND warehouse_id=? AND available_qty >= 1 LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED; (b) if row found: UPDATE inventory SET reserved_qty = reserved_qty + 1, available_qty = available_qty - 1 WHERE id=?. The primary-key point update in step (b) takes only a record lock, not a gap lock. SKIP LOCKED makes out-of-stock queries return immediately (no row found) without blocking, eliminating all deadlock paths. Wrap both in one @Transactional method.
5. Validate: After deploying READ COMMITTED: CloudWatch MySQL:Deadlocks drops from 1,420/min to <5/min within 3 minutes. DMLLatency P99 returns from 8.7s to <50ms. Order success rate climbs from 65.3% to >99.5%. Confirm in SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS: 'LATEST DETECTED DEADLOCK' timestamp should be >10 min old. Check performance_schema.events_statements_summary_by_digest for the UPDATE — avg_timer_wait should return to <500µs.
6. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm: MySQL:Deadlocks > 10/min for 2 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P2. (b) Load-test flash sale scenarios against a staging RDS clone with production-like concurrency (10K threads) — this deadlock was latent and only manifested at scale. (c) Add a deadlock integration test in the CI pipeline: 200 concurrent threads attempt to reserve the same SKU with available_qty=1; assert exactly 1 succeeds and the rest return a clean out-of-stock response (no 1213 errors). (d) Document transaction isolation policy in the architecture decision record: default new write-heavy service transactions to READ COMMITTED; reserve REPEATABLE READ for read-heavy transactions requiring consistent snapshot semantics. (e) Add HikariCP saturation alert: when pool.ActiveConnections / pool.TotalConnections > 85% for 1 minute → PagerDuty P2 'DB pool saturation'.

items.drill: Redis Sentinel Split-Brain — Dual Primary Writes During AWS AZ Partial Partition
items.layer: Database (Redis 7.2 Sentinel / Python FastAPI / AWS ECS Fargate)
items.scenario:
At 02:09:14 UTC, a custom integrity monitor fires PagerDuty: 'payment-service-prod dual-primary-write-conflict detected'. Your Python FastAPI payment service (AWS ECS Fargate, 24 tasks, us-east-1) uses Redis 7.2 Sentinel for idempotency key storage — a distributed lock preventing double-charges on payment mutations. Topology: 1 Primary (10.0.1.25:6379, us-east-1a) + 2 Replicas (10.0.1.26:6379 in us-east-1b, 10.0.1.27:6379 in us-east-1c) + 3 Sentinels (one per AZ), sentinel down-after-milliseconds=30000, quorum=2.

At 02:07:52 UTC, an AWS network event caused ~48 seconds of packet loss (>90% drop) between us-east-1a and us-east-1b/us-east-1c. The Primary in us-east-1a became unreachable from Sentinels in us-east-1b and 1c. After 30s, those two Sentinels declared the primary down and promoted the us-east-1b replica at 02:08:27 UTC:

  2026-05-18T02:08:22.341Z [1234] +sdown master mymaster 10.0.1.25 6379
  2026-05-18T02:08:26.891Z [1234] +odown master mymaster 10.0.1.25 6379 #quorum 2/2
  2026-05-18T02:08:26.896Z [1234] +try-failover master mymaster 10.0.1.25 6379
  2026-05-18T02:08:27.109Z [1234] +promoted-slave slave 10.0.1.26:6379 10.0.1.26 6379 @ mymaster 10.0.1.25 6379
  2026-05-18T02:08:29.887Z [1234] +failover-end master mymaster 10.0.1.25 6379

The original Primary (us-east-1a) was never unreachable to application tasks — 6 of 24 ECS tasks in us-east-1a stayed connected to it. Python redis-py Sentinel clients only refresh master address on ConnectionError, not proactively. Since us-east-1a's primary stayed alive, those 6 tasks had no error and continued routing writes there. From 02:08:35 to 02:10:41 UTC (126 seconds), two primaries accepted simultaneous writes:
  Primary A (us-east-1a, 10.0.1.25:6379): 847 SET idempotency_key commands
  Primary B (us-east-1b, 10.0.1.26:6379): 2,134 SET idempotency_key commands

When the AWS network recovered at 02:10:41 UTC, Primary A was demoted and underwent full resync from Primary B (2.4 GB RDB snapshot), permanently overwriting its 847 idempotency key records. redis-cli INFO replication on former Primary A post-recovery:
  role:slave
  master_host:10.0.1.26
  master_port:6379
  master_link_status:up
  master_sync_in_progress:0

23 payment requests had their idempotency keys written exclusively to Primary A. When those clients retried (their request timed out due to a brief latency spike), the retry landed on an ECS task connected to Primary B — which had no record of the idempotency key — and processed the payment a second time. Total duplicate charges: $47,200 across 23 transactions ($2,052 average). Business impact: regulatory breach notification required, emergency fraud team mobilized at 02:15 UTC, $47,200 immediate refund liability, potential PCI-DSS incident filing.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose blast radius: Reconstruct which idempotency keys were exclusively written to Primary A between 02:08:35–02:10:41 UTC. Pull Redis MONITOR replay from CloudWatch Logs (enable keyspace notifications via CONFIG SET notify-keyspace-events 'KxE'). Query payment service logs via CloudWatch Logs Insights: `fields @timestamp, payment_id, idempotency_key | filter @message like /SET idempotency/ and ecs_az='us-east-1a' and @timestamp between datetime(2026-05-18T02:08:35Z) and datetime(2026-05-18T02:10:41Z)`. Map resulting payment_ids against Stripe webhooks showing duplicate charge.succeeded events — these are the 23 duplicate charges.
2. Financial remediation: Issue refunds for duplicates via Stripe API: `stripe charges refund <charge_id> --reason=duplicate`. Preserve full audit trail (Redis logs, ECS task logs, Sentinel logs, AWS network event timestamps) for PCI-DSS incident report. File AWS Support case for AZ network event timeline confirmation — required for the post-mortem.
3. Immediate prevention — min-replicas-to-write: Add to Redis config on the Primary: `CONFIG SET min-replicas-to-write 1; CONFIG SET min-replicas-max-lag 10; CONFIG REWRITE`. This requires at least 1 replica to acknowledge writes within 10 seconds. During the AZ partition, Primary A loses all replica heartbeats within 10s and stops accepting writes — blocking any dual-write scenario entirely. Verify: run iptables -I OUTPUT -d 10.0.1.26 -j DROP on the Primary host — after 10s, SET commands return NOREPLICAS Not enough good replicas to write.
4. Increase sentinel down-after-milliseconds: Change 30,000ms → 60,000ms in sentinel.conf: `sentinel down-after-milliseconds mymaster 60000`. A 48-second AZ blip should not trigger a Sentinel failover at a fintech company. 60s tolerance absorbs routine inter-AZ transient events. Apply via rolling restart of all 3 Sentinel instances (Sentinel is stateful — always roll one at a time with 5s gap).
5. Validate split-brain protection in staging: (a) Start a write loop: `while true; do redis-cli SET idempotency:test:$(uuidgen) 1 EX 300; done`. (b) Block all replicas from Primary: iptables -I OUTPUT -p tcp --dport 6379 -j DROP on the Primary host. (c) After 10s, assert Primary returns NOREPLICAS — no idempotency key writes accepted. (d) Restore iptables, verify Primary resumes writes after replica reconnect and no duplicate writes occurred. Document this as a monthly chaos test in the runbook.
6. Prevent: (a) Add Datadog alert on redis.sentinel.failover.triggered — any Sentinel failover fires PagerDuty P2 + triggers an automatic 60-second payment processing freeze (new charge mutations paused until topology confirmed stable). (b) Add a secondary idempotency layer at the payment gateway: use Stripe's native Idempotency-Key header for all charge mutations — Stripe's own dedup survives Redis failures entirely. (c) Evaluate Redis Cluster as a replacement: Cluster's slot-based MOVED redirect prevents old-master write acceptance (cluster bus topology updates propagate within 100ms, all nodes know who owns each slot). (d) Add monthly chaos game day: 15-second iptables block on Primary replicas, assert NOREPLICAS within 10s, verify no data divergence post-recovery.

items.drill: GCP Cloud Bigtable Timestamp-Prefix Row Key Hotspot — Sequential Write Concentration Saturates Tablet Servers, Feature Store P99 Latency 20× SLO
items.layer: Database (Cloud Bigtable / GCP / Apache Beam / Dataflow / Python)
items.scenario:
At 09:44 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'ml-feature-store-prod bigtable-latency-p99 4,210ms (threshold: 200ms)'. Your Cloud Bigtable cluster (ml-feature-store-prod, us-central1, 6-node SSD cluster, ~2.2TB hot data) stores user behavior features for 3 real-time ML recommendation models at a P99 < 200ms SLO. The Apache Beam / Cloud Dataflow ingestion pipeline (24 n2-highcpu-32 workers) writes 85,000 rows/sec. Row key format chosen by the original author for 'time-ordered scans':

  # pipeline.py:88
  row_key = f"{int(time.time() * 1000)}#{user_id}#{event_type}"
  table.direct_row(row_key).set_cell(
    column_family_id='features', column='feature_vector', value=feature_bytes)

GCP Cloud Monitoring at 09:44 UTC:
  bigtable.googleapis.com/server/latencies p99: 12ms → 4,210ms (spike onset: 09:39 UTC)
  bigtable.googleapis.com/server/cpu/count_cpu_seconds_per_node:
    node-1 (us-central1-a): 96%
    node-2 (us-central1-a): 89%
    node-3 (us-central1-b):  7%
    node-4 (us-central1-b):  8%
    node-5 (us-central1-f):  6%
    node-6 (us-central1-f):  7%
  bigtable.googleapis.com/server/request_count by node:
    node-1: 61,847 req/s  |  node-2: 18,943 req/s  |  nodes 3–6: ~1,100 req/s each

GCP Console → Cloud Bigtable → Key Visualizer (10-min heatmap): a vivid red hotspot covers 0.03% of the key range at the lexicographic maximum (highest timestamp prefix). Every write for the current second lands in this sliver, owned exclusively by tablets on node-1 and node-2.

Root cause: Bigtable distributes row key ranges across tablet servers lexicographically. A monotonically increasing timestamp prefix means ALL current writes always target the highest possible key range — the same small set of tablets on 2 nodes. Node-3 through node-6 are idle because older timestamp ranges receive zero traffic. Additionally, ML model feature reads (`SELECT latest features WHERE user_id = ?`) cannot exploit the key structure; each read requires scanning across multiple tablets since user_id is not the leading key component, amplifying read latency further.

At the time of incident a new marketing campaign drove a 2.1× write-rate increase (85K → 179K rows/sec from 09:38 UTC), pushing the two hot tablet servers past their I/O ceiling. Error: `com.google.bigtable.v2.BigtableException: RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: Bigtable table 'user_features' has exceeded throughput limit.`

Business impact: 3 ML recommendation models fall back to Redis stale-feature cache (8-minute-old snapshots). CTR drops 6.8%, conversion −2.1%. Feature freshness SLA breached for 4 enterprise customers (guaranteed P99 freshness < 30s). Revenue impact estimated $190K over the 31-minute incident window.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm tablet hotspot vs network or client issue: GCP Console → Bigtable → ml-feature-store-prod → Monitoring → 'CPU utilization per node' panel — 2 nodes at >90%, 4 nodes at <10% confirms intra-cluster imbalance, not a capacity or network event. Open Key Visualizer (Console → Bigtable → Key Visualizer → select table → past 10 min) — a bright red column at the top of the key range is the smoking gun. Rule out client-side write skew: all 24 Dataflow workers emit writes to Bigtable uniformly by user_id; the hotspot is in Bigtable's key routing, not in client partitioning.
2. Immediate mitigation: (a) Throttle the Dataflow ingestion pipeline: update the Dataflow job FlexRS config with `--maxWriteRowsPerSecond=30000` via the Dataflow UpdateJob API — reduces node-1/node-2 CPU from 96% → ~45% within 3 min, restoring sub-200ms latency. (b) Flip the ML model read path to Redis fallback explicitly via a feature flag (`USE_REDIS_FALLBACK=true` environment variable) to stop latency degradation from propagating to end users. (c) Do NOT attempt to add nodes as the first response — adding nodes only shifts key ranges and does not fix the root cause; with a timestamp prefix the new nodes' ranges will be empty (historical keys) while current-time keys stay concentrated.
3. Root cause — timestamp-prefix anti-pattern: GCP Bigtable documentation explicitly states: 'Don't use timestamps as the first element of your row key.' Bigtable tablet servers each own a lexicographic contiguous range. A monotonically increasing first key component means 100% of all writes for any given second go to the same tablet. This is the most common Bigtable schema mistake. Verify: run `cbt -instance=ml-feature-store-prod read user_features prefix="$(python3 -c 'import time; print(int(time.time()*1000))')" limit=10` — all 10 rows should have nearly identical timestamps, confirming the concentration.
4. Key redesign — user_id-first with reversed timestamp for recency ordering: New row key: `{user_id}#{reversed_timestamp_ms:019d}#{event_type}` where reversed_timestamp = 9223372036854775807 − unix_timestamp_ms. This achieves two goals simultaneously: (a) Distributes writes across all user_ids (high cardinality, ~80M distinct values → uniform spread across all 6 nodes). (b) Preserves per-user recency ordering — reversed timestamp sorts newest row first within each user's key range, enabling efficient `Prefix(user_id#)` scans for 'latest feature' reads. Code change (pipeline.py:88): `MAX_LONG = 9223372036854775807; reversed_ts = MAX_LONG - int(time.time() * 1000); row_key = f"{user_id}#{reversed_ts:019d}#{event_type}"`
5. Zero-downtime migration — dual-write with table cutover: (a) Deploy the new Beam pipeline writing to `user_features_v2` (new key format) in addition to legacy `user_features` — dual-write for 24h. (b) Validate v2 key distribution: Key Visualizer on `user_features_v2` should show a symmetric heat gradient across the full key range within 15 min. (c) Switch ML model reads to `user_features_v2` via feature flag once P99 latency on the new table is confirmed < 15ms. (d) Disable writes to legacy table after 24h. (e) Drop `user_features` after 7 days (confirm no active read traffic via Cloud Monitoring request_count).
6. Validate: After the new Beam pipeline goes live with reversed-timestamp keys: Key Visualizer on `user_features_v2` shows uniform heatmap (no isolated red column). Node CPU: all 6 nodes at 14–18% (balanced). bigtable.googleapis.com/server/latencies p99 drops to < 15ms within 5 min of first batches using the new key format. ML models switch to `user_features_v2` — feature freshness returns to < 30s (SLA restored). Confirm per-user reads are efficient: `cbt read user_features_v2 prefix=\"<sample_user_id>#\" limit=5` returns the 5 most recent feature rows for that user without cross-tablet scan.
7. Prevent: (a) Add Cloud Monitoring alert on Bigtable node CPU coefficient of variation > 0.4 (high std-dev across nodes indicates hotspot): custom metric computed via Cloud Function polling per-node CPU → PagerDuty P2 'Bigtable node CPU imbalance'. (b) Add Key Visualizer screenshot to the monthly Bigtable health review — a symmetric heatmap is healthy; asymmetric bright columns require immediate key design audit. (c) Enforce schema review in the table ADR: any new Bigtable table must document the row key design and pass the question 'Is the leading key component monotonically increasing?' — if yes, block table creation until redesigned with a distributed prefix (user_id, hash, or reversed timestamp). (d) Add a load-test step to the Dataflow pipeline CI: run 5-min benchmark at 2× peak write rate against a staging Bigtable instance, assert stddev(per-node CPU) < 0.15 — catches timestamp-prefix hotspot before production deploy.

items.drill: DNS Resolution Failure
items.scenario: Internal service discovery starts returning NXDOMAIN for service-b.internal. Half of service-a instances can't reach service-b. The other half work fine because they have cached DNS entries.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Set up CoreDNS or dnsmasq, remove a record, observe partial failure
2. Diagnose: Check DNS TTL, which pods have cached vs expired entries, dig/nslookup
3. Hotfix: Restore DNS record, force flush caches on affected pods
4. Validate: All instances resolve correctly, no more connection errors
5. Harden: Lower DNS TTL for critical services, add DNS monitoring, client-side retry with backoff

items.drill: AWS EKS VPC CNI Subnet IP Exhaustion — Silent Scale Failure During Spot Interruption Wave
items.scenario:
At 14:52 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'checkout-service-prod pod-availability 62% (threshold: 90%)'. Your EKS 1.29 cluster (us-east-1a, node group checkout-nodes, m6i.4xlarge Spot, min=6 max=20, Cluster Autoscaler v1.29.0) processes $3.1M/hr for a Go checkout service. At 14:48 UTC, AWS issues spot interruption 2-minute eviction notices to 6 of 10 running nodes simultaneously (us-east-1a capacity-constrained — competing reservation from neighboring tenant). Cluster Autoscaler attempts to provision 6 replacement nodes. All 6 EC2 RunInstances calls fail, logged every 10s:

  W0424 14:49:04.101847 1 aws_cloud_provider.go:728] Failed to create instance in group checkout-nodes-asg-useast1a: InsufficientFreeAddressesInSubnet: Not enough free addresses in subnet subnet-0f4a8d21c to fulfill your request.
  [22 additional identical failures over 4 minutes]

kubectl get pods -n checkout shows 6 pods stuck Pending:
  Warning  FailedScheduling: 0/4 nodes are available: 4 Insufficient cpu. preemption: 0/4 nodes are available...

Root cause: Subnet subnet-0f4a8d21c (10.10.4.0/24, 251 usable IPs) was provisioned 2 years ago when the cluster had 4 nodes. With VPC CNI WARM_IP_TARGET=10 and MINIMUM_IP_TARGET=16, each m6i.4xlarge pre-allocates 16 secondary IPs (1 primary ENI + 2 secondary ENIs × warm pool). At 10 active nodes: 10 × 16 = 160 IPs consumed. The 6 interrupted nodes' ENIs are still detaching (60–90s teardown delay), holding 96 IPs in limbo. Effective free IPs at scale-up attempt: 251 − 160 − 96 + 60 (still releasing) = ~55 free — below the 96 needed for 6 new nodes. EC2 ASG batch launch fails all-or-nothing. No alert fires — there is zero CloudWatch monitoring on subnet AvailableIpAddressCount. Business impact: checkout error rate 38%, $1.2M revenue at risk over the 30-minute window.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm subnet exhaustion vs spot capacity failure — error codes are distinct: InsufficientFreeAddressesInSubnet (VPC/networking) vs InsufficientInstanceCapacity (EC2 compute). Run: aws ec2 describe-subnets --subnet-ids subnet-0f4a8d21c --query 'Subnets[0].AvailableIpAddressCount' — expect a small number (<20). Verify detaching ENIs holding IPs: aws ec2 describe-network-interfaces --filters 'Name=subnet-id,Values=subnet-0f4a8d21c' 'Name=status,Values=detaching' | jq '.[].NetworkInterfaceId'. Cross-check CA logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system deployment/cluster-autoscaler | grep InsufficientFreeAddresses.
2. Immediate mitigation: Associate the node group with a second pre-existing /22 subnet (1,000+ IPs) that's been reserved for expansion: aws eks update-nodegroup-config --cluster-name prod-eks --nodegroup-name checkout-nodes --subnets subnet-0f4a8d21c subnet-0b8e9f33d. CA will pick up the new subnet on its next 10s retry cycle and launch nodes in subnet-0b8e9f33d. Alternatively set 4 On-Demand base capacity in the ASG MixedInstancesPolicy to restore minimum capacity while spot scaling is blocked.
3. Release trapped IPs: Wait for detaching ENIs to fully release (monitor aws ec2 describe-network-interfaces — status transitions from 'detaching' to absent). Do NOT force-detach; this can orphan IPs in VPC state. Available IPs will increase by ~96 within 90s of node termination completing. Verify with aws ec2 describe-subnets after release.
4. Validate: Watch kubectl get nodes -w — new nodes should appear Running within 90s of successful ASG launch. Checkout pods transition Pending→Running. Error rate returns to <0.5% within 3 min of new nodes becoming Ready. Confirm AvailableIpAddressCount drops correctly after node join (IPs allocated, not leaked).
5. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm on subnet IP exhaustion — custom metric via Lambda polling aws ec2 describe-subnets — alert at <50 IPs (warning) and <20 (critical). (b) Always associate node groups with 2+ subnets — CA round-robins across them on launch failures. (c) Switch to VPC CNI prefix delegation (ENABLE_PREFIX_DELEGATION=true, WARM_PREFIX_TARGET=1) — assigns /28 blocks per ENI, reducing per-node IP consumption from 16 IPs to 2 ENI slots, enabling 16× denser packing in a /24. (d) Monthly capacity review: aws ec2 describe-subnets --query 'Subnets[?AvailableIpAddressCount<`50`].[SubnetId,AvailableIpAddressCount]'.

items.drill: Certificate Expiry Outage
items.scenario: Friday 11pm: your TLS cert expires. All HTTPS traffic gets ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID. Customers see security warnings. Automated renewal failed silently 3 days ago.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Generate a self-signed cert with 1-minute expiry, serve HTTPS, watch it fail
2. Diagnose: Check cert expiry with openssl s_client, review cert-manager logs
3. Hotfix: Manually renew cert, update load balancer / ingress, verify with curl
4. Validate: HTTPS works, cert chain is valid, no mixed content
5. Prevent: Alert on certs expiring within 14 days, test renewal in staging monthly

items.drill: Container OOMKilled in Production
items.scenario: Pods keep getting OOMKilled. Kubernetes restarts them but they crash again within minutes. Resource limits are set to 512MB but the app needs 800MB under load due to a recent feature adding image processing.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Deploy an app with memory limit 256MB, trigger load that exceeds it
2. Diagnose: kubectl describe pod (OOMKilled reason), check resource requests vs actual usage
3. Hotfix: Increase memory limit, rolling restart, verify pods stabilize
4. Validate: Monitor memory usage under load, no more OOMKill events
5. Root fix: Profile the image processing — stream instead of buffer, add memory-bounded queue

items.drill: Deployment Rollback Under Pressure
items.scenario: New deploy shipped 5 minutes ago. Error rate jumped from 0.1% to 15%. PagerDuty firing. The deploy has a database migration that already ran. Simple kubectl rollout undo won't work.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Deploy v2 with a bug + a migration that adds a column, observe errors
2. Diagnose: Confirm error spike correlates with deploy timestamp, identify the breaking change
3. Hotfix: Feature-flag the new code path off (v2 stays deployed but old behavior restored)
4. Validate: Error rate drops, new column is unused but harmless
5. Design: Expand-contract migration pattern so rollbacks are always safe

items.drill: Vault Dynamic Secret max_ttl Reduction — Synchronized Lease Expiry Causes Mass PostgreSQL Auth Failure
items.layer: Infrastructure (HashiCorp Vault 1.15 / Go / pgx v5 / AWS EKS / PostgreSQL 15 RDS)
items.scenario:
At 01:03 UTC, PagerDuty fires seven simultaneous alerts within 90 seconds: 'user-service-prod db_connection_failure_rate 100%', 'order-service-prod db_connection_failure_rate 100%', and five more across all Go microservices. Your 12 Go services (8 pods each = 96 pods on AWS EKS us-east-1, c6i.2xlarge) connect to PostgreSQL 15 RDS Multi-AZ (db.r6g.4xlarge, us-east-1) via HashiCorp Vault 1.15 PostgreSQL secrets engine. Each pod runs a vault-agent sidecar (vault:1.15.2 image) that writes short-lived DB credentials to /vault/secrets/db-creds.json. Go services use a pgx v5 connection pool initialized once at startup.

Vault operator change log at 2026-05-21T00:00:00 UTC:

  $ vault write database/roles/user-service-role \
    db_name=rds-prod \
    creation_statements='CREATE ROLE "{{name}}" WITH LOGIN PASSWORD ''{{password}}'' VALID UNTIL ''{{expiration}}''; GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO "{{name}}";' \
    default_ttl=3600 \
    max_ttl=3600
  # Changed from max_ttl=86400 (24h) → 3600 (1h) to tighten security posture

The operator did not realize: when max_ttl is shortened below existing lease remaining-TTL, Vault caps each lease at the new max_ttl on its NEXT renewal attempt. All 96 pods' vault-agent leases — spread across 23 hours of issue times — had remaining TTL > 1h at 00:00 UTC. Exactly 1 hour later at 01:00 UTC, Vault refuses to renew all 96 leases simultaneously (renewal past max_ttl is rejected). vault-agent logs on every pod:

  2026-05-21T01:00:03.241Z [ERROR] agent.auth.handler: error renewing token: error="context deadline exceeded"
  2026-05-21T01:00:03.244Z [ERROR] agent.template.render: lease renewal failed for database/creds/user-service-role, lease_id=database/creds/user-service-role/8KzP3x
  2026-05-21T01:00:04.871Z [INFO]  agent.auth.handler: authenticating — performing new Kubernetes auth login
  2026-05-21T01:00:05.113Z [INFO]  agent.template.render: writing new credentials to /vault/secrets/db-creds.json (username=v-user-svc-7ef2b)

vault-agent successfully re-authenticates and writes NEW credentials to disk within 2 seconds. However, the Go service's pgx pool was initialized in main() using the OLD credentials at startup:

  // main.go:94
  creds, _ := ioutil.ReadFile("/vault/secrets/db-creds.json")
  pool, _ = pgxpool.New(ctx, fmt.Sprintf(
    "postgresql://%s:%s@rds-prod-useast1.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/userdb",
    creds.Username, creds.Password))

Vault revokes all expired leases (automatic on max_ttl breach), dropping the PostgreSQL roles v-user-svc-9cd4a, v-order-svc-b3f17, ... from RDS. Existing pgx pool TCP connections — still alive per TCP keepalive — now fail on every query. RDS PostgreSQL logs:

  2026-05-21T01:00:06.312Z [29847] FATAL:  password authentication failed for user "v-user-svc-9cd4a"
  [96 pods × 25 pool connections attempting reconnect = 2,400 auth failures/sec for 6 minutes]

RDS CloudWatch (01:00–01:06 UTC):
  DatabaseConnections: drops from 2,400 → 0 (all connections rejected)
  LoginFailures: 14,400 over the 6-minute window
  CPUUtilization: 8% (low — DB is fine, auth layer is rejecting before SQL executes)
  FreeableMemory: Normal

New connections using the NEW Vault credentials (v-user-svc-7ef2b — written to /vault/secrets/db-creds.json at 01:00:05) also fail — pgx pool is a static object created with old credentials; it does not watch the file for changes and does not reinitialize automatically. The only resolution path is a pod restart. All 7 services: HTTP 500 on every request. 340K active user sessions error out. Payment processing queue backs up 186K orders. Business impact: $2.1M/min revenue halt for 22 minutes; SLA breach for all 14 enterprise customers.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — differentiate credential failure from network failure: kubectl exec -it user-service-6f9b4-0 -n prod -c app -- psql 'postgresql://rds-prod-useast1.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/userdb?user=probe&password=probe' — FATAL: password authentication failed (not a connection refused or timeout) confirms auth layer is the issue, not network or RDS health. Check vault-agent sidecar logs: kubectl logs user-service-6f9b4-0 -c vault-agent -n prod --since=10m | grep -E 'ERROR|renew|writing' — confirms lease renewal failure at 01:00:03 and new credential write at 01:00:05 UTC. Read current credentials from the pod: kubectl exec -it user-service-6f9b4-0 -c app -n prod -- cat /vault/secrets/db-creds.json — shows username=v-user-svc-7ef2b (new creds). Confirm pgx pool was started with OLD creds: kubectl logs user-service-6f9b4-0 -c app -n prod | grep 'db pool initialized' — shows 'connecting as v-user-svc-9cd4a' from startup time (22+ hours ago). This proves: fresh creds are available on disk, but the Go pool is static.
2. Before restarting — verify Vault can issue new credentials: vault read database/creds/user-service-role — must return HTTP 200 with a valid username/password. If this fails (Vault itself unhealthy, unsealed, or database connection broken), fix Vault first or pods will restart into the same error. Also verify the new PostgreSQL role exists on RDS: kubectl exec -it user-service-6f9b4-0 -c app -n prod -- psql 'postgresql://v-user-svc-7ef2b:<password>@rds-prod-useast1...' — must connect successfully before triggering the rolling restart.
3. Immediate mitigation — rolling pod restart across all 12 services: for each service, run: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<service-name> -n prod. Use --max-surge=2 --max-unavailable=1. Each pod restarts, reads /vault/secrets/db-creds.json (current valid credentials), initializes a fresh pgx pool with v-user-svc-7ef2b credentials, and begins serving traffic. First pods recover within 30–60s. All 12 services recover in ~8 minutes. Monitor: watch kubectl get pods -n prod to confirm Ready status climbing service by service.
4. Root cause — two compounding bugs: (a) Policy change risk: Vault documents that shortening max_ttl caps all renewal attempts at T+max_ttl from the policy change timestamp, not from the original lease issue time. With 96 leases distributed across 23h, all hit the new 1h ceiling simultaneously at 01:00 UTC — a synchronized mass expiry. The operator had no tooling to preview how many leases would expire and when. Check: vault list sys/leases/lookup/database/creds/user-service-role to count active leases before making max_ttl changes. (b) Static connection pool: pgx pool initialization in main() reads credentials once. The vault-agent template writes new credentials silently but the application has no hook to reinitialize the pool on file change.
5. Fix — hot credential rotation without restart: Replace the static pool initialization with a file-watching pool reconnect handler. Add fsnotify watcher in main.go: go watchVaultCreds(ctx, '/vault/secrets/db-creds.json', &poolRef). When the file changes (vault-agent writes new creds), watchVaultCreds calls poolRef.Close() then reinitializes poolRef with the new credentials and logs 'pool reconnected with rotated credentials'. Use atomic.Value to swap the pool reference safely under concurrent requests. Alternatively, configure vault-agent to send SIGHUP to PID 1 on credential write: add exec { command = ['sh', '-c', 'kill -HUP 1'] } in the vault-agent template stanza, and add a signal.Notify(sigCh, syscall.SIGHUP) handler in main() that triggers graceful pool rotation.
6. Validate hot rotation: In staging, deploy the fsnotify fix. Run: vault write database/roles/user-service-role max_ttl=60 (60s max_ttl). Wait 60s — vault-agent rotates credentials. Assert: kubectl logs user-service-<pod> -c app | grep 'pool reconnected' appears within 10s. kubectl logs user-service-<pod> -c app | grep 'password authentication failed' returns zero lines. Run k6 load test at 200 RPS during rotation — assert zero errors throughout the credential swap.
7. Prevent: (a) Vault lease impact preview: Before any max_ttl reduction, run: vault list sys/leases/lookup/database/creds/<role> and vault read sys/leases/lookup/database/creds/<role>/<lease_id> to see expiry times. Stagger if >10 leases expire within the same 60s window by first renewing leases individually: vault lease renew -increment=7200 database/creds/user-service-role/<lease_id> — adds 2h buffer before the new max_ttl takes effect per lease. (b) Alert on mass lease expiry: add a Prometheus metric vault_agent_credential_age_seconds (last renewal timestamp) per pod — alert if stddev(vault_agent_credential_age_seconds across all pods) < 30s AND count > 5 (synchronized expiry signal). (c) Add a Vault runbook: Rotating max_ttl on production roles requires a maintenance window ticket, preview of active lease count, and staggered lease pre-renewal before the policy write. (d) Integration test: In CI (using vault-test-fixtures or a LocalStack-equivalent Vault dev server), deploy the Go service, force vault-agent credential rotation via vault write -f database/rotate-root/rds-prod, and assert zero HTTP 5xx responses from the service during the rotation — catches static pool regression on every PR that touches vault-agent config or main.go database initialization.

items.drill: Triton Inference Server Silent Fallback — Model Config/Weight Shape Mismatch
items.layer: ML Serving (Triton / AWS EKS / SageMaker Feature Store)
items.scenario:
At 09:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'fraud-model-triton error_rate 12.3% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Triton Inference Server cluster (6× NVIDIA T4-16GB on AWS EKS, us-east-1) serves a fraud detection ONNX model at /v2/models/fraud_detector/infer. The model was automatically retrained overnight (weekly cycle) and new weights uploaded to s3://ml-models-prod/fraud_detector/v2.3.1/. Triton polls S3 every 30 min for hot-reloads. Triton logs flood with:

  E0409 09:03:14.221847 1 model_repository_manager.cc:1741] failed to load 'fraud_detector' version 3: [StatusCode::INVALID_ARGUMENT] model config input 'input__0': the model expect tensor shape [1,512] but the configuration specified shape [1,256]
  E0409 09:03:14.221859 1 model_repository_manager.cc:1744] ModelRepositoryManager::LoadModels() failed for model 'fraud_detector'

Triton silently fell back to v2.3.0 (old weights, 256-dim input), but the upstream SageMaker Feature Store pipeline was already migrated to produce 512-dim feature vectors for v2.3.1. 12% of requests hit a code path that passes new vectors to the old model, causing shape mismatch errors. The remaining 88% still hit the legacy feature path and serve stale predictions from a 6-day-old model. Business alert: chargeback rate jumped from 0.8% → 1.2% ($280K daily revenue impact). The CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions) shows the model export job succeeded but config.pbtxt was never updated — a shape detection bug in the config generation script failed silently on dynamic ONNX axes.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Check nv_inference_request_failure and nv_model_load_duration_us in Prometheus. GET /v2/models/fraud_detector to confirm which version is READY (expect v2 = READY, v3 = UNAVAILABLE). Check CloudWatch Logs for S3 sync events — confirm weights arrived but config update failed.
2. Immediate mitigation: Roll back the SageMaker Feature Group to produce 256-dim vectors (flip feature pipeline version env var, redeploy SageMaker Processing Job). This restores consistency with the old weights. Error rate should drop to ~0% within 2 min as new feature batches flow through.
3. Root cause: Inspect config.pbtxt in s3://ml-models-prod/fraud_detector/v2.3.1/ — input shape still shows [1,256]. Review generate_config.py in CI: the dynamic axis detection calls onnx.checker.check_model() but reads input shape from the first symbolic dim, which is 'batch_size', not the feature dim. Bug introduced in commit a3f8d2c.
4. Fix: Correct generate_config.py to read input shape from dim[1] instead of dim[0]. Regenerate config.pbtxt with correct shape [1,512], upload both config and weights to S3. Trigger manual reload: POST /v2/repository/models/fraud_detector/load (body: {"parameters": {"config": ""}}). Wait for model version 3 status = READY.
5. Validate: GET /v2/models/fraud_detector/versions/3 → READY. Re-enable 512-dim feature pipeline. Confirm nv_inference_request_failure drops to 0. Monitor chargeback rate returns to 0.8% baseline within 2–4 hours.
6. Prevent: Add model config validation step in GitHub Actions before S3 upload — compare all config.pbtxt input/output shapes against actual ONNX graph shapes using onnx.load().graph.input[i].type.tensor_type.shape. Alert on any Triton model load failure event (not just on inference errors), and add a liveness check that validates the expected model version is READY after every retraining pipeline run.

items.drill: vLLM KV-Cache Exhaustion Under Traffic Burst
items.scenario: At 14:23 PST, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-serving-prod error rate 28.4% (threshold: 5%)'. Your vLLM cluster (4× A100-80GB, GKE) serves Llama-3-70B via /v1/completions. Grafana shows `vllm:gpu_kv_cache_usage_perc` at 99.8% across all nodes, `vllm:num_queued_requests` spiking to 340. Logs flood with: `torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.34 GiB (GPU 0; 79.20 GiB total capacity; 71.42 GiB already allocated; 2.01 GiB free; 72.18 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)`. Concurrency jumped from 18→52 req/s after a marketing email blast at 14:15 PST. P99 latency: 1.2s → 47s timeout. Model weights (35 GB) are fine — KV cache for in-flight sequences consumed the remaining VRAM.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Query Prometheus for vllm:num_running_requests, vllm:num_queued_requests, vllm:gpu_kv_cache_usage_perc — confirm KV cache is the bottleneck, not model weights or CUDA fragmentation
2. Hotfix: Patch vLLM deployment ConfigMap — reduce max_num_seqs from 256→48 and max_num_batched_tokens from 8192→4096. Rolling-restart pods one at a time (kubectl rollout restart, 1 unavailable) to preserve partial capacity
3. Load shedding: Apply nginx rate limit (limit_req_zone, 40 r/s burst=20) at the Istio ingress gateway to prevent re-saturation during restarts
4. Validate: KV cache utilization stabilizes at ~65%, P99 returns to 1.6s, error rate < 0.8% within 8 minutes of first pod restart completing
5. Root fix: Calculate correct max_num_seqs = floor((total_gpu_mem - model_weights_mem - overhead) / (max_model_len × num_layers × head_dim × 2 × dtype_bytes)). Add HPA rule: scale out when vllm:num_queued_requests > 15 for 60s. Add pre-scaling hook tied to marketing campaign scheduler.

items.drill: Ray Serve Head Node Preemption — Stale GCS Actor Handles Zombie All GPU Workers
items.layer: ML Serving (Ray Serve / Azure AKS / Python)
items.scenario:
At 03:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'mistral-inference-prod error_rate 100% for 6 minutes (threshold: 5%)'. Your Ray 2.10.0 cluster (1 head node + 8 worker nodes on Azure AKS, Standard_NC24ads_A100_v4 spot instances, eastus2) serves a fine-tuned Mistral-7B-Instruct model via Ray Serve at /v1/chat/completions. At 03:12:07 UTC, Azure preempted the head node spot VM (30-second IMDS eviction notice received at 03:11:37 UTC, missed by on-call). AKS restarted the head pod in 72 seconds. Serve controller log:

  2026-04-21T03:12:07Z WARN  RayletClient - Lost connection to GCS at ray-head-svc.ray-system.svc.cluster.local:6379
  2026-04-21T03:13:19Z INFO  GcsClient - Reconnected to GCS at ray-head-svc.ray-system.svc.cluster.local:6379 (new epoch: 15)
  2026-04-21T03:13:22Z ERROR ServeController - Failed to recover deployment mistral_serve: ActorID b7f2a3c18f4e1d20... is unreachable
  2026-04-21T03:14:05Z ERROR ServeController - Replica mistral_serve#replica_0: actor handle stale (registered GCS epoch 14, current epoch 15)
  2026-04-21T03:14:05Z ERROR ServeController - Replica mistral_serve#replica_1: actor handle stale (registered GCS epoch 14, current epoch 15)
  [6 more identical lines for replicas 2–7]

All 8 workers still hold PyTorch CUDA contexts from GCS epoch 14. nvidia-smi on every worker shows zombie python processes: GPU-Mem=36400MiB/40960MiB, GPU-Util=0%, State=C. New actor scheduling fails: ray.exceptions.GetTimeoutError: Failed to submit task to actor b7f2a3c1... after 30.0s: No healthy replicas. Ray Dashboard: mistral_serve status=UNHEALTHY, running_replicas=0, pending_replicas=8 (stuck STARTING for 4+ min). 14,200 active WebSocket sessions receive 503 ServiceUnavailable. Business impact: $22,800 revenue loss on a $0.08/request pay-per-use API; SLA breach for 3 enterprise customers.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: kubectl get pods -n ray-system — head pod shows RestartCount=1, Running for 5 min. kubectl exec -it ray-head-0 -n ray-system -- ray status — reports 8 nodes registered, 0 live actors. On each worker: kubectl exec -it ray-worker-0 -n ray-system -- nvidia-smi — confirms zombie python pids with 36.4 GB allocated but 0% GPU compute. This proves GCS epoch mismatch: workers hold epoch-14 actor PIDs that no longer exist in the head's fresh epoch-15 registry.
2. Immediate mitigation: kubectl rollout restart daemonset/ray-worker -n ray-system. Each worker restart kills zombie python processes (releasing 36.4 GB GPU memory per node) and re-registers with GCS epoch 15. Ray Serve controller detects newly healthy workers and schedules 8 fresh replicas. Mistral weights reload from Azure Blob Storage model cache: ~12s if cached on node, ~35s for cold nodes.
3. Validate: Watch replica count recover: watch -n 5 'kubectl exec ray-head-0 -n ray-system -- ray status | grep running_replicas'. Confirm it climbs 0→1→...→8 over 3–4 minutes. Send a test request: curl -s -X POST https://mistral-prod.eastus2.cloudapp.azure.com/v1/chat/completions -d '{"model":"mistral-7b-instruct","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' — expect 200 OK with valid JSON. Error rate drops to 0 within 30s of 8th replica becoming HEALTHY.
4. Root cause: Ray GCS state was stored in-memory only (RAY_gcs_storage=memory, the default). On head pod restart, GCS starts fresh with epoch 15. Workers still running hold Python object references (actor handles) to specific GCS-epoch-14 remote process PIDs — those PIDs are gone. Ray 2.10 does not automatically evict cross-epoch zombie actors from worker CUDA contexts; only a worker-process restart flushes them. The IMDS eviction notice at 03:11:37 went unmonitored, giving 30s that was not used to drain traffic.
5. Long-term fix — Redis GCS persistence: Configure KubeRay RayCluster CR with a Redis sidecar on the head pod and set RAY_gcs_storage=redis, GCS_REDIS_ADDRESS=localhost:6379. With GCS persistence enabled, head pod restart replays GCS state — workers re-register with the same epoch, actors recover in-place in <10s with zero GPU memory teardown.
6. Long-term fix — Evict head from spot pool: Migrate the head node pool to on-demand (az aks nodepool update --name rayhead --priority Regular --resource-group ml-prod-rg --cluster-name aks-ml-east2). Worker pools remain spot to save cost; head node stability eliminates the #1 GCS state-loss vector at ~$180/month extra.
7. Prevent: (a) Add Azure Monitor alert on IMDS scheduledevents API returning EventType=Preempt targeting head node pool — fire PagerDuty P2 with 25s lead time to trigger graceful drain. (b) Add synthetic health check every 60s: curl /v1/models and assert 200 OK + running_replicas=8 — alert on first failure, not user 503s. (c) Add Grafana alert: ray_serve_deployment_replica_running_replicas{deployment='mistral_serve'} < 8 for 60s → PagerDuty P1. (d) Add CI regression test: kubectl delete pod -l ray.io/node-type=head and assert serve recovery within 3 min (Redis GCS) or 5 min (worker rollout fallback).

items.drill: TorchServe Worker Zombie — CUDA Context Poisoned by OOV Embedding Lookup
items.scenario:
At 08:44 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'recommendation-endpoint-prod InvocationModelErrors 100% for 4 minutes (threshold: 2%)'. Your SageMaker real-time endpoint (ml.g4dn.2xlarge, 1× NVIDIA T4-16GB, TorchServe 0.9.0, model: RecoBERT-v2.1 product recommendation) has served cleanly for 4 months. CloudWatch dashboard shows: ModelLatency=0ms, GPUUtilization=0%, GPUMemoryUtilization=92.5% (14.8 GB / 16 GB — still fully allocated), CPUUtilization=0.3%. Container logs flood with:

  2026-04-17T08:43:11Z [ERROR] BaseHandler - CUDA error: device-side assert triggered at model forward pass: index 3902 is out of bounds for dimension 0 with size 3800
  2026-04-17T08:43:11Z [ERROR] WorkerThread - RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered
  2026-04-17T08:43:12Z [INFO] ManagementRequestHandler - Worker recovery: restarting pid=2844
  2026-04-17T08:43:13Z [ERROR] Worker-2 pid=2847 - RuntimeError: Failed to initialize CUDA context: CUDA error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed): context is destroyed
  2026-04-17T08:43:15Z [ERROR] Worker-3 pid=2851 - RuntimeError: Failed to initialize CUDA context: CUDA error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed): context is destroyed

Worker pid=2844 hit a CUDA device-side assert when a product category embedding lookup used index 3902 — beyond RecoBERT-v2.1's vocabulary size of 3800. The triggering event: an upstream catalog service deployed 103 new product category IDs at 08:43:10 UTC, expanding from category_id 3799 to 3902 without notifying downstream ML consumers. TorchServe's recovery logic spawned 3 new workers, but the CUDA context for cuda:0 entered a fatal error state. New workers cannot create a fresh CUDA context on the same device. Zombie pid=2844 holds 14.8 GB GPU memory but shows no active PID in nvidia-smi (process is Z/defunct). The endpoint serves zero successful requests for 22 minutes. Business impact: 340K recommendation API calls failed during peak morning traffic; A/B test results corrupted for 3 running experiments.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Query CloudWatch Logs Insights: `fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /index.*out of bounds/ | sort @timestamp asc | limit 1` — pinpoints first CUDA assert at 08:43:11. Cross-reference with upstream catalog service deployment event at 08:43:10 (check ECS deploy history or Spinnaker pipeline). Confirm zombie memory signature: GPUMemoryUtilization > 80% with GPUUtilization < 5% for > 3 min.
2. Immediate mitigation: SageMaker doesn't support per-container process restart — trigger endpoint update to cycle the fleet: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint --endpoint-name recommendation-endpoint-prod --endpoint-config-name <current-config>`. This destroys the poisoned CUDA context and releases 14.8 GB. Expect ~90s of full downtime while new containers start.
3. Root cause: RecoBERT-v2.1 embedding layer is `nn.Embedding(3800, 256)` in model.py. Any input with category_id > 3799 triggers an unhandled CUDA bounds error. `CUDA error: device-side assert triggered` is non-recoverable — it permanently corrupts the CUDA context on that device, making all subsequent CUDA allocations fail with error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed). The upstream catalog service had no ML schema contract defining the valid category_id range.
4. Short-term fix: Add bounds clamping in the TorchServe handler's `preprocess()` method before forward pass: `category_ids = torch.clamp(category_ids, 0, EMBEDDING_VOCAB_SIZE - 1)`. Alternatively, map OOV IDs to a dedicated `<UNK>` token (ID=0). Deploy via `aws sagemaker create-model` + `update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities` for zero-downtime model swap.
5. Prevent: (a) Add a custom TorchServe health check handler that runs a forward pass with synthetic valid inputs every 30s — if CUDA context is poisoned, health check fails and SageMaker auto-replaces the instance before manual intervention is needed. (b) Publish a `MAX_CATEGORY_ID` contract to AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store; add a pre-deploy validation step in the catalog service CI that reads this param and rejects deploys that would exceed it. (c) Add CloudWatch alarm: GPUUtilization < 5% AND GPUMemoryUtilization > 80% for 3 consecutive minutes → PagerDuty P2 'zombie GPU context' alert.

items.drill: vLLM Speculative Decoding Draft Model Vocab Mismatch — Corrupted Responses After Target Model Rollout
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM 0.4.2 / GKE Autopilot / NVIDIA A100-80GB)
items.scenario:
At 19:23 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-gateway-prod synthetic-quality-monitor p99-response-length 8 tokens (threshold: 80 tokens) for 8 minutes'. Your vLLM 0.4.2 cluster (6× A100-80GB on GKE Autopilot, us-central1-a) serves Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct at https://llm-gateway-prod.internal/v1 at 3,100 RPS. Speculative decoding is enabled: draft model=Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (vocab_size=32,000), num_speculative_tokens=5. An Argo CD sync at 19:15 UTC upgraded the target model to Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-v2 (expanded multilingual tokenizer: 32,000→36,864 vocabulary). The draft model image was NOT updated — still vocab_size=32,000.

vLLM container logs (kubectl logs llm-serving-6d9f7-0 -n inference --since=15m):

  2026-05-06T19:21:07Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Spec decode acceptance: 0/5 (0.0%) seq_id=91847 [draft=llama-3-1-8b-instruct target=llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2]
  2026-05-06T19:21:08Z WARN  vllm.spec_decode.batch_expansion - SpecDecodeWorker: rejection on position 4 (last proposal token) — output_token_ids buffer ptr advanced without EOS consume [seq_id=91848]
  2026-05-06T19:21:09Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Generated 7 tokens for seq_id=91849 (stop_reason=EOS) duration=88ms
  2026-05-06T19:21:09Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Generated 9 tokens for seq_id=91850 (stop_reason=EOS) duration=91ms
  [repeating across all 6 pods]

Prometheus at 19:23 UTC:
  vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate{model="llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2"} = 0.00  (baseline: 0.71)
  vllm:generation_tokens_per_second = 290  (baseline: 4,180)
  vllm:num_running_requests = 240
  vllm:request_success_total rate = 3,100/s  (all HTTP 200 OK — no application-layer errors visible)

Sample API response: {"choices":[{"message":{"content":"certainly! photosynthesis occurs when"}}]} — 6 tokens, mid-sentence truncation.

Root cause — two compounding failures: (1) Token IDs 32,000–36,863 exist in the new tokenizer's multilingual expansion. The 8B draft model has an embedding matrix of size 32,000 — any proposal requiring one of these IDs results in 100% speculation rejection. In vLLM 0.4.2, rejection on the last speculative token slot (idx=4 = num_speculative_tokens−1) fails to reset the output_token_ids write pointer after advancing it for the rejected token. The next sequence begins writing at the already-advanced position, reading trailing garbage from the previous sequence's buffer and triggering a spurious EOS after 7–9 tokens. (2) The Argo CD Application manifest defines target and draft model as separate image tags in two different ConfigMap keys (TARGET_MODEL_IMAGE and SPEC_DECODE_MODEL_IMAGE). There is no deployment-time assertion that the two tokenizer vocabularies match.

14,800 API calls returned truncated 6–9 token responses before the synthetic quality monitor fired. Three enterprise customers on B2B SLAs triggered automated incident escalations. Business impact: $96K in SLA penalty credits; ML team's live A/B experiment results invalidated.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm spec-decode as root cause — check vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate in Grafana: dropped to 0.00 at 19:15 UTC (correlates with Argo CD sync). Sample 5 live responses: for i in $(seq 5); do curl -s -X POST https://llm-gateway-prod.internal/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Explain the water cycle in 200 words"}],"max_tokens":300}' | jq '.choices[0].message.content | length'; done — observe values consistently <15. Confirm vocab mismatch: kubectl exec llm-serving-6d9f7-0 -n inference -- python3 -c "from transformers import AutoTokenizer; d=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('/models/draft'); t=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('/models/target'); print('draft', d.vocab_size, 'target', t.vocab_size)" — prints draft 32000 target 36864.
2. Immediate mitigation: Disable speculative decoding — kubectl set env deployment/llm-serving -n inference VLLM_SPECULATIVE_MODEL='' VLLM_NUM_SPECULATIVE_TOKENS=0 then kubectl rollout restart deployment/llm-serving -n inference (max-unavailable=1). Each pod takes ~45s to reload weights from GCS. Throughput drops 4,180→1,200 tok/s but responses are correct. Confirm fix: after first restarted pod becomes Ready, re-run the 5-sample curl test — assert response word count >100. Update Argo CD Application to sync-paused to prevent auto-reverting the env override.
3. Root cause — vLLM 0.4.2 engine bug: In spec_decode/batch_expansion.py, the output buffer write pointer is incremented when a draft token is tentatively appended during proposal scoring. On full rejection (all 5 slots), the rollback path correctly resets pointer for positions 0–3 but skips position 4 (off-by-one in the rollback loop bound). This causes the pointer to remain advanced by one token position for the next sequence. The fix is upstream PR #4899 ('Fix off-by-one in spec decode rejection rollback loop') merged in vLLM 0.4.3.
4. Fix: Upgrade vLLM from 0.4.2 to 0.4.3+: update the container image tag in the Argo CD Application spec from vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.2 to vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.3. Simultaneously update the draft model to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-v2 (vocab_size=36,864, matching target). Re-enable speculative decoding with the corrected model pair. Validate via vllm-benchmark: python -m vllm.entrypoints.benchmark_throughput --model /models/target --speculative-model /models/draft --num-prompts 200 --output-len 200 — assert median output_len>190 tokens and spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate>0.60.
5. Validate: After full rollout, confirm vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate returns to 0.65–0.75 (slight drop from 0.71 baseline expected as 8B-v2 adapts to expanded vocab distribution). Replay 50 logged failing requests from Cloud Logging (filter: resource.type=k8s_container, jsonPayload.seq_id in [91847..92200]) against the fixed endpoint — assert all responses exceed 100 tokens. Run 24h shadow traffic comparison: A/B 5% canary with fixed build vs production.
6. Prevent: (a) Add Argo CD PreSync hook that asserts vocab parity: annotate the Rollout with argocd.argoproj.io/hook: PreSync — hook script: python3 /scripts/validate_model_pair.py which runs AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained() for both target and draft and asserts vocab_size equality; sync fails on mismatch. (b) Add Grafana alert: vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate < 0.20 for 90s → PagerDuty P1 'spec-decode model mismatch'. (c) Add a synthetic quality canary: every 60s POST a deterministic prompt 'Count from 1 to 30:' and assert len(response.split()) > 25 — this fires within 60s vs the 8-minute gap in this incident. (d) Store SPEC_DECODE_MODEL_PAIR as a single Kubernetes annotation on the Deployment (e.g., 'llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2:llama-3-1-8b-instruct-v2') — ArgoCD diff surfaces mismatches visually on every rollout plan review.

items.drill: NCCL AllReduce Deadlock — Tensor Parallel vLLM Silent Hang After EFA Driver Upgrade
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM 0.5.1 / AWS p4d.24xlarge / 8× A100-80GB / EFA Networking)
items.scenario:
At 22:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-serving-70b p99_latency 120,000ms (threshold: 5,000ms)' — but error_rate=0.0%. Every in-flight completions request hangs with an open connection; no bytes are ever written to the response body. Your vLLM 0.5.1 cluster (2× AWS p4d.24xlarge, 8× A100-80GB per node, tensor_parallel_size=8) serves Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct at /v1/completions at 380 RPS for an enterprise code-assistant product. AWS performed a scheduled maintenance window at 22:30 UTC and upgraded the Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) driver from aws-efa-installer-1.30.0 → 1.31.0 on both instances without a change notification to the ML team.

kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference --since=30m shows normal completions up to 22:44:03 UTC then silence. NCCL_DEBUG=INFO on rank 0:

  2026-05-15T22:44:07Z INFO  NCCL - ncclAllReduce: Using algorithm=TREE (size=268435456 bytes, threshold=67108864, afEFASupported=1)
  2026-05-15T22:44:09Z WARN  NCCL - rank 0: waiting for rank 5 in AllReduce [nflowsPerPeer=4, groupId=2] ... elapsed 2000ms
  2026-05-15T22:44:15Z WARN  NCCL - rank 0: waiting for rank 5 in AllReduce [nflowsPerPeer=4, groupId=2] ... elapsed 8000ms
  2026-05-15T22:44:29Z ERROR NCCL - ncclAllReduceKernel: timeout 22000ms. All 8 ranks blocked in double-binary-tree barrier. groupId=2, peerRank=5
