nvidia-smi on the p4d instance: all 8 GPUs show Process=python3 (vllm worker), Memory=78432/81920MiB (96%), GPU-Util=0% — no kernel is executing. All 8 ranks are blocked in the NCCL double-binary-tree (DBT) all-reduce barrier.

Root cause: In EFA driver 1.31.0, the efa kernel module changed efa_use_device_rdma default from 0 → 1, enabling Device RDMA. NCCL 2.21.5 (bundled in PyTorch 2.3.0, required by vLLM 0.5.1) added a path: when efa_use_device_rdma=1 is detected, all-reduce operations above 64 MB switch from Ring → Double Binary Tree (DBT), which uses a new 2-step flow-control handshake across 4 NCCL channels. However, vLLM 0.5.1's async_llm_engine.py dispatches the all-reduce on a dedicated nccl_stream per rank that is not fully synchronized with that rank's attention CUDA stream before the DBT pre-send barrier check. On rank 5 (the 6th A100 in the 8-way TP group), the attention output is computed on cuda:5's default stream. The DBT pre-send barrier fires on nccl_stream5 before the default-stream attention kernel has produced its output. Rank 5 stalls at the barrier waiting for an event that is posted on the default stream — but the default stream is also waiting for nccl_stream5 to release a semaphore. Circular dependency: all 8 ranks deadlock. The RPC path continues to accept connections (HTTP layer is healthy) but all generation workers are frozen, so no response bytes ever complete. Business impact: 380 RPS × 22 minutes = 501,600 failed requests; 3 enterprise B2B customers trigger SLA credit calculations ($186K exposure); 74,000 inference jobs queue in the upstream pipeline.
items.exercise:
1. Distinguish deadlock from OOM: nvidia-smi shows 96% VRAM allocated with GPU-Util=0% on all 8 GPUs — an OOM crash would show a dead process (0 MiB) and a pod in CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl get pods -n inference shows Running (not CrashLoop). This is a live deadlock: processes are alive but stuck. Confirm NCCL hang: kubectl exec llm-serving-0 -n inference -- kill -3 <vllm-pid> (SIGQUIT triggers Python traceback): all worker threads blocked in ncclAllReduceKernel. Check NCCL log: kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference | grep -E 'TREE|RING|timeout|rank 5' — see TREE algorithm selected at 22:44:07, timeout at 22:44:29.
2. Identify EFA driver as root cause: SSH to the p4d instance (or kubectl exec into the node-debug pod if using privileged DaemonSet): modinfo efa | grep efa_use_device_rdma — shows 'parm: efa_use_device_rdma:Enable device RDMA (default: 1 [changed in 1.31.0])'. Confirm version: apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep aws-efa-installer → 'aws-efa-installer/now 1.31.0'. Cross-reference with AWS EFA 1.31.0 release notes (aws.amazon.com/hpc/efa): 'efa_use_device_rdma now defaults to enabled.' This caused NCCL to switch from Ring → DBT for the 256-MB all-reduces vLLM performs during attention projection.
3. Immediate mitigation: Force NCCL to use Ring algorithm: kubectl set env deployment/llm-serving -n inference NCCL_ALGO=Ring NCCL_TREE_THRESHOLD=0. Rolling restart: kubectl rollout restart deployment/llm-serving -n inference (maxUnavailable=0, maxSurge=1). Each new pod starts with NCCL_ALGO=Ring — the DBT path is never taken, no stream synchronization issue. Deadlocked pods are terminated, releasing all 8 A100 CUDA contexts and draining hung HTTP connections. Monitor: kubectl rollout status deployment/llm-serving -n inference — first Ready pod in ~2 min.
4. Validate fix: After first new pod is Ready, run: for i in $(seq 20); do curl -s -w '%{http_code} %{time_total}\n' -X POST https://llm-prod.internal/v1/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"llama-3-1-70b-instruct","prompt":"Hello","max_tokens":20}' -o /dev/null; done — assert all HTTP 200, time_total < 4.0s. Confirm NCCL log: kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference | grep 'algorithm=' — should show 'Using algorithm=RING' for all allReduce ops. Check Prometheus: vllm:generation_tokens_per_second should return to ~45,000 tok/s (baseline).
5. Long-term engine fix: vLLM async_llm_engine.py must synchronize each rank's attention output stream with the NCCL stream before the all-reduce call, regardless of the NCCL algorithm: add torch.cuda.current_stream(device).synchronize() before dispatcher._run_workers('execute_model', ...) in the TP path. File upstream vLLM GitHub issue: 'NCCL DBT pre-send barrier deadlock under EFA efa_use_device_rdma=1 (aws-efa-installer 1.31+, NCCL 2.21.5, PyTorch 2.3.0).' Track fix in vLLM 0.5.2+. Until upstream fix lands, keep NCCL_ALGO=Ring pinned.
6. Infrastructure hardening: (a) Pin EFA driver in the AWS Launch Template AMI: add UserData script assert: efa_version=$(modinfo efa | awk '/^version/{print $2}'); [[ "$efa_version" == '1.30.0' ]] || (echo 'EFA driver version mismatch — aborting AMI promotion' && exit 1). Block AMI promotion in CI until a 2-min NCCL health test passes: nccl_tests all_reduce_perf -b 64M -e 512M -f 2 -g 8 — a deadlock would cause this to hang and fail the job. (b) Add a deadlock-specific Prometheus alert: vllm:request_latency_p99 > 30000 AND vllm:error_rate < 0.01 for 3 min → PagerDuty P1 'LLM serving silent hang'. Standard error_rate alerts completely miss this class of failure — the symptom is high latency, not errors. (c) Add a NCCL liveness check to the Kubernetes readinessProbe: a 30s periodic exec probe runs python3 -c 'import torch.distributed as dist; dist.all_reduce(torch.zeros(1).cuda())' with a 10s timeout — pod is marked NotReady if the probe hangs, triggering a rolling replacement before user traffic is impacted.

items.drill: SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoint LRU Cache Thrashing — EU/US Time-Zone Traffic Shift Causes P99 Latency 28s
items.layer: ML Serving (AWS SageMaker MME / Python FastAPI / XGBoost / AWS)
items.scenario:
At 09:02 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'personalization-api-prod p99-latency-breach 28.4s (threshold: 2.0s)'. Your SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoint (MME) `personalization-mme-prod` (8x ml.g5.2xlarge instances, 24 GB GPU each, us-east-1) hosts 1,847 per-tenant XGBoost ranking models (avg 420 MB loaded in memory, 340 MB on disk in S3). SageMaker MME's built-in LRU cache holds ~55 models per instance (8 x 55 = 440 total warm slots across the fleet). EU tenant accounts (100-800) drive 51% of daily traffic; US accounts (1,000-1,847) dominate overnight.

At 09:00 UTC, the EU business day starts. EU models have been cold (evicted overnight) since 17:00 UTC the prior day. When EU traffic spikes from 0 to 18,000 req/min in 4 minutes:

  2026-05-29T09:00:14.221Z [INFO] sagemaker-mme: ModelLoadingStarted model=tenant_287_ranking_v3.tar.gz instance=personalization-mme-prod-ml-g5-2xlarge-0
  2026-05-29T09:00:14.889Z [INFO] sagemaker-mme: ModelEvicting model=tenant_1247_ranking_v2.tar.gz (LRU, last_access=2026-05-28T23:47:02Z)
  [... 1,847 eviction/load cycles across 8 instances over 6 minutes ...]

Each model load from S3 takes 4.8-7.2 seconds (340 MB download at ~50 MB/s + XGBoost deserialization). SageMaker MME load latency adds directly to inference latency from the caller's perspective -- the HTTP connection blocks until model loading completes. Download is single-threaded per instance. During the cache thrashing window, all 8 instances are simultaneously loading EU models while evicting US models.

CloudWatch metrics (09:00-09:09 UTC):
  SageMaker/ModelLoadingTime avg: 5.8s
  SageMaker/ModelUnloadingCount: 847 in first 6 minutes (normal: 0-2/hr)
  SageMaker/InvocationModelLoadingTime: 92% of all invocations contain model load overhead (normal: < 1%)
  SageMaker/InvocationsPerInstance P99: 4,200/min (near endpoint concurrency limit of 4,000)
  SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate: 7% (normal: 98%)
  SageMaker/EndpointLatency P99: 28.4s (SLO: 2.0s)

Root cause: The MME LRU cache capacity (55 models x 8 instances = 440 warm slots) is insufficient for the 1,847-model fleet when two geographic traffic cohorts swap simultaneously. The daily EU/US boundary creates a predictable eviction storm that wasn't captured by any alarm. The FastAPI gateway (httpx client, timeout=10s) times out only 36% of requests; the other 64% wait the full 28.4s P99, holding open TCP connections and exhausting the upstream connection pool.

Business impact: personalization API P99 exceeds SLA for 142 EU enterprise tenants for 9 minutes. FastAPI httpx clients trigger static ranking fallback for top-percentile requests, degrading CTR 14.2% for EU tenants. $340K at-risk subscription revenue (SLA breach triggers credits). Two EU tenants open severity-1 tickets before automated alerts fire.
items.exercise:
1. Triage -- confirm model load overhead (not GPU OOM or CPU saturation): CloudWatch Metrics -> SageMaker/InvocationModelLoadingTime -- 92% of invocations at 09:01 UTC. Confirm cache miss: SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate drops to 7%. Rule out instance saturation: CPUUtilization < 45%, MemoryUtilization < 62%. Check CloudTrail S3 GetObject latency: avg 4.9s for 340 MB tar.gz files -- normal for us-east-1 inter-AZ S3 throughput. This confirms bottleneck is sequential model cold-loading, not compute.
2. Immediate mitigation -- pre-warm EU models in parallel: Issue dummy inference requests for each EU tenant account to force model loading outside the production hot path. `aws sagemaker-runtime invoke-endpoint --endpoint-name personalization-mme-prod --target-model tenant_{id}_ranking_v3.tar.gz --body '{}' --content-type application/json /tmp/out.json`. Run in parallel across all 440 warm slots using asyncio: 440 models x 5.8s avg / 8 instances = ~40 concurrent loads per instance. Monitor: SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate climbs back to >90% within ~5 min of warm-up completing.
3. Scale endpoint to increase cache capacity: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities --endpoint-name personalization-mme-prod --desired-weights-and-capacities '[{"VariantName":"AllTraffic","DesiredInstanceCount":16}]'`. Doubling from 8->16 instances doubles warm-model slots to 880 -- enough to hold EU + US cohorts simultaneously (700 EU + 847 US = 1,547 < 880 with some eviction, or size to 34 instances for full coverage at 1,870 slots). New instances join in ~4 min (ml.g5.2xlarge start time).
4. Durable fix -- scheduled pre-warming via EventBridge + Lambda: (a) Rule 1: fires at 08:45 UTC daily -> Lambda warms EU tenant accounts 100-800 (700 models, ~5 min to warm across 8 instances at 40 concurrent loads/instance). (b) Rule 2: fires at 17:00 UTC daily -> warm US accounts 1,000-1,847. Lambda code: `asyncio.gather(*[invoke_endpoint(id) for id in tenant_ids], return_exceptions=True)` with MaxConcurrency=400 matching endpoint capacity. Add structured CloudWatch Logs for each warm-up invocation to audit pre-warm coverage.
5. Validate: Trigger manual EventBridge at 08:55 UTC in staging. Monitor SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate -- expect >95% for EU tenant range within 5 min. Replay 1,000 EU tenant inference requests at 09:00 -- P99 should stay < 500ms (pure inference, no load overhead). Confirm ModelUnloadingCount stays < 50 during EU ramp-up.
6. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm: ModelCacheHitRate < 80% for 2 consecutive 1-min periods -> PagerDuty P2 'MME cache hit rate degraded'. (b) Add alarm: ModelLoadingTime avg > 2s for 3 consecutive minutes -> PagerDuty P2. (c) Daily Lambda computes (peak_concurrent_tenants x avg_model_size) / (instances x instance_memory) and pages if ratio > 70%. (d) Evaluate SageMaker MME with NVIDIA Triton backend -- Triton's GPU model cache is 10x larger per instance, reducing cold-load frequency. (e) Long-term: migrate hot tenants to dedicated endpoints (InferenceGraph/KServe), keeping the MME only for the long tail of infrequent-access tenants.

items.drill: TGI Streaming Zombie Sessions — iOS Background TCP Suspension Exhausts KV Cache Pool
items.layer: ML Serving (text-generation-inference 2.1.0 / AWS SageMaker / Rust / iOS)
items.scenario:
At 09:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'chat-api-prod SageMakerInvocationErrors 89.3% (threshold: 5%)'. Your SageMaker real-time endpoint `mistral-chat-prod` (4× ml.g5.4xlarge, 1× NVIDIA A10G-24GB each, text-generation-inference 2.1.0) serves Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 for a mobile chat application. Model weights consume 14.1 GB; the paged-attention KV cache pool is allocated the remaining ~8.5 GB (~228 pages of 128 tokens each). At peak load each streaming session holds roughly 300 MB of KV cache pages.

At 05:00 UTC, iOS app version 4.2.0 shipped a background-fetch feature. When the iOS system suspends the app mid-session (e.g., incoming phone call, lock screen), it suspends the underlying TCP socket without sending a FIN or RST packet — the kernel TCP window simply shrinks to 0. Over 4 hours and 14 minutes, 2,847 such suspended sockets accumulate: users opened an SSE `/generate_stream` request and then backgrounded the app.

TGI's Rust streaming path in `router/src/server.rs` uses `axum::response::Sse::new(stream).keep_alive(KeepAliveConfig::default())`. `KeepAliveConfig::default()` sends a `:keepalive\n\n` SSE comment every 15s — but these comments queue into the Tokio `StreamWriter` internal buffer and are never flushed when the kernel TCP send window is 0. The underlying `poll_write` on `tokio_util::io::StreamWriter` returns `Poll::Pending` indefinitely (EAGAIN on the socket). Tokio parks the async task and never applies a timeout: no `tokio::time::timeout()` wraps the send path.

Each zombie Tokio task in `router/src/infer.rs` holds a `CacheEntry` — a live reference into the paged-attention KV cache pool — for the task's lifetime. The task is alive but blocked on TCP write and never returns its cache pages. By 09:14 UTC:
  2,847 zombie sessions × avg 2.97 MB KV cache = 8.46 GB consumed (entire 8.5 GB KV pool exhausted)

SageMaker CloudWatch at 09:14 UTC:
  GPUMemoryUtilization: 99.8% (23.95 GB / 24 GB)
  GPUUtilization: 2.3% (almost no generation running — only keepalive path alive)
  InvocationErrors: 89.3% (previously 0.0%)
  ModelLatency: 0ms (requests fail before reaching the model)

TGI container logs via CloudWatch Logs at 09:14:03 UTC:
  ERRO text_generation_router::infer - Scheduler error: InferError::Overloaded(\"Queue is full (max_waiting_tokens=20000, current=20000)\")
  ERRO text_generation_router::server - Generate stream error: Overloaded (\"Queue is full\")
  WARN text_generation_router::scheduler - Batch slots exhausted: all 228 KV-cache pages allocated (page_size=128tokens, kv_cache_total_pages=228, kv_cache_free_pages=0)
  ERRO text_generation_router::infer - Rejected request req_id=b9f82a3 reason=\"No free KV cache pages\"

`ss -tnp` on the container (via SageMaker SSM session) shows 2,847 ESTABLISHED connections with remote TCP receive window = 0 — the iOS suspended-socket signature. None appear in TGI's `tgi_request_count` or `tgi_request_success` Prometheus metrics because they are Tokio tasks blocked on TCP write, never completing generation. Standard metrics show 0 active requests, 0 errors, and healthy queue sizes — only GPUMemoryUtilization at 99.8% with near-zero GPU compute is anomalous.

Business impact: 89.3% of chat API requests fail for 31 minutes. 47,000 active mobile sessions receive HTTP 500. 4 enterprise B2B customers breach their 99.9% monthly uptime SLA ($156K penalty exposure). The on-call spends 19 minutes investigating the application layer before correlating the iOS 4.2.0 deploy with the GPU memory signature.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm zombie session signature (not OOM or model bug): Check `tgi_queue_size` and `tgi_request_count{status='success'}` in CloudWatch — both near 0 despite 99.8% GPUMemoryUtilization. This 'high VRAM, low utilization, zero active requests' pattern uniquely identifies KV cache held by zombie tasks (a real OOM crash would show a dead process, 0 MiB allocated, and a pod in CrashLoopBackOff). Confirm via container SSM session: `ss -tnp | awk '$3==0{c++}END{print c}'` — count of ESTABLISHED sockets with zero TCP receive queue reveals the 2,847 suspended iOS connections. Rule out a model bug: a fresh container restart will immediately free all KV pages (all zombie Tokio tasks killed), confirming external TCP state — not model or routing logic — is holding the cache.
2. Immediate mitigation — cycle SageMaker containers to flush all zombie tasks: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint --endpoint-name mistral-chat-prod --endpoint-config-name mistral-chat-prod-config-20260601`. This terminates all 4 containers, kills all 2,847 zombie Tokio tasks, releases the full KV cache pool, and restarts TGI with a clean slate. Expect ~90s of full downtime. For a lower-impact option, add 2 clean instances via `update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities`, drain traffic from zombie instances to the new ones, then remove the zombie instances — achieving near-zero-downtime recovery at the cost of 4 extra minutes.
3. Immediate config fix — add `--client-idle-timeout` (TGI 2.2.0+): Deploy TGI 2.2.0 image (upgrade from 2.1.0) and add startup flag: `TGI_ARGS=--client-idle-timeout 30`. This flag terminates any streaming session where the TCP write buffer has been full for >30s, sending an SSE error event and releasing the KV cache pages. Deploy via SageMaker model update (update container environment variable) — zombie accumulation cannot restart until the next iOS 4.2.0 backgrounding event, giving a ~4h reprieve.
4. Root cause — Rust `poll_write` EAGAIN is valid backpressure; Tokio respects it indefinitely: In TGI 2.1.0, `router/src/server.rs:generate_stream_handler` uses `axum::response::Sse` backed by `tokio_util::io::StreamWriter`. When the iOS kernel TCP window reaches 0, every `StreamWriter::poll_write()` returns `Poll::Pending` (EAGAIN). Tokio's cooperative scheduler parks the task — a legitimate I/O-wait state. No `tokio::time::timeout()` wraps the write path, so the task waits forever. `KeepAliveConfig::default()` sends SSE `:keepalive` comments every 15s but only enqueues them into the Tokio send buffer — they do not probe the TCP socket reachability. Confirmed via `ss --processes --tcp state established` showing 2,847 connections all with `Send-Q > 0` (data queued, never draining).
5. Validate recovery: After container restart and TGI 2.2.0 deploy, run: `curl -N -H 'Content-Type: application/json' https://chat-api.internal/generate_stream -d '{"inputs":"ping","parameters":{"max_new_tokens":50}}'` — confirm SSE tokens stream normally. Monitor `GPUMemoryUtilization` — should return to ~62% (model weights + small active-request KV) within 2 minutes. Validate zombie protection: using `tc netem` simulate a TCP black-hole (`iptables -A OUTPUT -m statistic --mode nth --every 1 -j DROP` on the client side), connect an SSE stream, and assert TGI logs `client idle timeout after 30s` and releases the KV cache page — confirm `tgi_cache_utilization` drops within 35s.
6. Prevent: (a) Add a compound CloudWatch alarm: `GPUMemoryUtilization > 90% AND GPUUtilization < 10%` for 5 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P1 'TGI zombie KV cache accumulation'. A normal high-load signature shows BOTH metrics elevated; this combination exclusively identifies zombie sessions. (b) Pin TGI to 2.2.0+ in the SageMaker model container ARN — add a CI check that rejects any model package with `tgi==2.1.x` in the container label. (c) Fix iOS app 4.2.1: register for `UIApplicationDidEnterBackgroundNotification` and explicitly cancel the URLSessionDataTask — `task.cancel()` sends a TCP FIN packet, allowing TGI to cleanly close the Tokio task and release KV pages within 100ms. (d) Add a monthly chaos canary: simulate 200 iOS TCP-suspension connections (using `iptables -I INPUT -s <client-ip> -j DROP`) and assert `GPUMemoryUtilization` stabilizes to baseline within `--client-idle-timeout + 10s`; alert if the KV cache does not recover, catching regression if the idle-timeout flag is accidentally removed.

items.drill: Azure Cosmos DB Hot Partition — RU Exhaustion on Viral Traffic
items.scenario: At 16:42 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'cosmos-prod request-rate-too-large 23.7% (threshold: 1%)'. Your Azure Cosmos DB (West US 2, SQL API) stores 2B+ documents serving a social feed service. Partition key is user_id, container provisioned throughput is 50,000 RU/s. A viral post from a celebrity account (user_id: usr_0XF8A2, 42M followers) generates 180K requests/minute. All hit the same physical partition. SDK errors flood: CosmosException: Status 429 (TooManyRequests), SubStatusCode: 3200 (RequestRateTooLarge), RetryAfter: 2000ms, ActivityId: d4f1e8a3-9c2b-47e1-b3f5-8a190c344d21. Azure Monitor shows partition range 15 consuming 78% of total RU, starving all other tenants. P99 feed-load latency: 340ms → 8.2s. Business alert: engagement drop 34%, estimated $190K/hr ad revenue impact.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Query Azure Monitor Diagnostic Logs — table CDBPartitionKeyRUConsumption — filter PartitionKeyRangeId='15', sum(RequestCharge) by 1-min bins. Confirm 1% of partitions consuming >70% of provisioned RU. Cross-check with CDBDataPlaneRequests to identify top callers (feed-reader service + notification fanout jobs).
2. Immediate mitigation: Upgrade container from fixed 50K RU to autoscale (10K–100K RU). This is a live change, zero downtime, completes in <2 min. Buys breathing room while designing durable fix.
3. Short-term cache bypass: Deploy a Redis read-through cache for top-500 hot accounts (sorted set by follower count, refreshed every 60s). Route GET /feed?user_id={celebrity} to Redis with 5s TTL. Cache hit ratio drops hot-partition reads by ~85% within 3 min of rollout.
4. Root cause analysis: partition_key=user_id is an anti-pattern for high-cardinality celebrities. Fan-out writes (followers' timeline updates) + fan-out reads both land on the same physical shard. Review SDK request logs — 2,200 reads/s and 180 writes/s both targeting usr_0XF8A2.
5. Root fix: Redesign partition key to composite: user_id|date_bucket (e.g., 'usr_0XF8A2|20260410'). Distribute temporal reads across multiple partitions. For accounts with follower_count > 500K, pre-compute feed via fan-out-on-write (write to each follower's timeline at post creation, not at read time). Store celebrity feeds in a separate high-throughput container (200K RU autoscale).
6. Prevent: Add 'celebrity_tier' flag in user metadata. Route flagged accounts to hot-path container at onboarding. Alert rule: any PartitionKeyRangeId consuming >50% of container RU for >90s triggers PagerDuty P1.

items.drill: Cassandra Compaction Storm During Write-Heavy Migration
items.scenario:
At 02:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'cassandra-prod-us-east1 read-latency-p99 4210ms (threshold: 200ms)'. Your Cassandra 4.1.3 cluster (6× GCP c2-standard-60 nodes, us-east1, 2.1TB total data) serves a user-session store at ~120K ops/sec. An overnight migration job wrote 840M new rows between 22:00–02:00 UTC via a Spark job, flooding the cluster with SSTables. By 02:14, SizeTieredCompactionStrategy triggered concurrent major compaction on all 6 nodes simultaneously (compaction score >1.0):

  $ nodetool compactionstats
  pending tasks: 312
  keyspace=sessions, table=user_state: 44 sstables compacting (STCS major), estimated 18.4GB remaining

  $ nodetool tpstats | grep ReadStage
  ReadStage          active:3  pending:2847  completed:1203401  blocked:94

  GC logs (GCP ops-agent → Cloud Logging):
  2026-04-13T02:14:22.831Z GC(4731) Pause Full (System.gc()) 28156M->18422M(29696M) 4.211s
  2026-04-13T02:14:30.993Z GC(4732) Pause Full (System.gc()) 27891M->18107M(29696M) 4.008s

  Application error rate: 18.4%. Coordinator returning:
  ReadTimeoutException: code=1200 [Coordinator node timed out waiting for replica response]
  consistency=LOCAL_QUORUM (2 responses required but only 0 received, data_present=false)

  GCP Monitoring shows sustained disk write throughput 1.19 GB/s per node, saturating local SSD write bandwidth (cap: 1.2 GB/s). CPU on all nodes: 92% iowait.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Run nodetool compactionstats on all nodes — confirm concurrent compaction is the bottleneck, not replication lag or GC pressure alone. Check nodetool cfstats sessions.user_state for SSTable count (>50 SSTables = STCS threshold breach). Cross-reference GCP Monitoring disk write throughput to confirm I/O saturation.
2. Immediate mitigation: Throttle compaction I/O to free up read bandwidth: nodetool setcompactionthroughput 32 (MB/s, down from unlimited). This reduces disk write pressure, allowing reads to recover within 3–5 minutes. Simultaneously: nodetool setstreamthroughput 50 to prevent any repair jobs from competing.
3. Stop the bleeding: Identify the migration Spark job — check GCP Dataproc job status. If still writing, pause or rate-limit the job (add BATCH_SIZE throttle, target <5K rows/sec). This prevents new SSTable creation from extending the compaction backlog.
4. Address GC: The CMS Full GC is triggered by compaction holding large SSTable references. Connect via jmx and call System.gc() reduction via jvm_opts: add -XX:+UseG1GC, -Xmx20G, -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=300. Stage rolling JVM restarts one node at a time (nodetool drain first): wait for ReadStage pending to drop below 50 before restarting next.
5. Validate recovery: Monitor nodetool compactionstats — pending tasks should drop below 50 within 15 min. P99 read latency should return to <200ms within 20 min. Error rate should drop to <0.1%.
6. Root fix and prevent: Switch compaction strategy to LeveledCompactionStrategy (LCS) for this table — it bounds SSTable count to max 10 per level and never triggers write-amplification storms. Alter: ALTER TABLE sessions.user_state WITH compaction = {'class': 'LeveledCompactionStrategy', 'sstable_size_in_mb': 160}. For future migrations: add a nodetool import path instead of Spark direct-write — this lets Cassandra ingest SSTables at controlled rate. Alert on: nodetool cfstats sstable_count > 32 for any table, and compaction pending tasks > 100.

items.drill: Kafka Consumer Livelock — max.poll.interval.ms Breach During Downstream Throttle
items.scenario:
At 11:34 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'kafka-consumer-lag-prod notification-pipeline-consumer-group 3.7M messages (threshold: 100K)'. Your Java Spring Boot notification pipeline (6 consumer instances on AWS EKS, us-east-2) processes the notification-events topic (120 partitions, 3 brokers, replication-factor=3). Kafka broker metrics show group rebalances occurring every 2–3 minutes. Consumer logs flood with:

  [consumer-3] WARN ConsumerCoordinator - [clientId=notification-consumer-3, groupId=notification-pipeline] Synchronous commit failed: Commit cannot be completed since the group has already rebalanced and assigned the partitions to another member. This means that the time between subsequent calls to poll() was longer than the configured max.poll.interval.ms, which is 300000. This interval can be controlled by increasing the consumer's `max.poll.interval.ms` property.
  [consumer-3] INFO AbstractCoordinator - [clientId=notification-consumer-3, groupId=notification-pipeline] (Re-)joining group
  [consumer-3] INFO AbstractCoordinator - [clientId=notification-consumer-3, groupId=notification-pipeline] Successfully joined group with generation 1847

  Rebalance cycle repeating every 280s across all 6 pods.

Root cause: A SendGrid API incident at 10:45 UTC silently raised per-email delivery latency from 48ms → 11.2s average (their status page showed no incident). Notification service config: max.poll.records=500, max.poll.interval.ms=300000 (5 min). Processing a 500-record batch now takes 500 × 11.2s = 5600s — 18× the poll interval. Every consumer gets ejected from the group mid-batch, rejoins, gets assigned partitions, starts processing the same batch, gets ejected again. Livelock: zero messages committed. Consumer lag growing at 195K messages/minute. Business impact: 2.4M users not receiving order/shipment notifications, CS ticket volume +340%, estimated SLA breach in 22 minutes.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Check Kafka JMX metric kafka.consumer:type=consumer-coordinator-metrics,name=rebalance-rate-per-hour — confirm it is ~20 rebalances/hr (healthy baseline: <2). Run: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server kafka-prod.us-east-2.internal:9092 --describe --group notification-pipeline — all members show LAG growing, CONSUMER-ID cycling each time you refresh. Confirm no Kafka broker issues: kafka.server:type=ReplicaManager,name=UnderReplicatedPartitions should be 0.
2. Immediate mitigation: Reduce max.poll.records from 500 → 5 via ConfigMap update — this cuts worst-case batch processing time to 5 × 11.2s = 56s, well within max.poll.interval.ms=300s. Rolling-restart all 6 pods (kubectl rollout restart deployment/notification-consumer). Consumers will resume committing offsets within 2–3 minutes of restart. Lag growth stops.
3. Isolate SendGrid degradation: Add a circuit breaker (Resilience4j CircuitBreaker) on the SendGrid HTTP client with slowCallDurationThreshold=2000ms and slowCallRateThreshold=50%. When tripped, route to a local retry queue (SQS FIFO dead-letter) instead of blocking the consumer thread. This prevents a downstream throttle from ever violating the Kafka poll contract again.
4. Root cause: Review consumer config — max.poll.records=500 was tuned for 48ms/record (expected throughput: ~10K records/min). There is no monitoring on per-record processing latency or SendGrid response time. Kafka consumer lag alert threshold of 100K was set when processing was fast; at 195K/min growth rate, the alert fired too late (lag was already 3.7M by the time PagerDuty notified).
5. Validate recovery: After rolling restart with max.poll.records=5 — monitor kafka.consumer:type=consumer-fetch-manager-metrics,name=records-lag-max across all partitions. Should drop from 3.7M over 20–30 min. Rebalance rate should return to <2/hr. Confirm offset commits succeeding: kafka.consumer:type=consumer-coordinator-metrics,name=commit-latency-avg < 50ms.
6. Prevent: (a) Add downstream-latency-aware poll sizing: export a Prometheus metric notify_send_duration_seconds and add a JVM hook that dynamically reduces max.poll.records when P99 send latency exceeds 500ms. (b) Set max.poll.interval.ms = 3 × expected_max_batch_duration with 2× safety margin. (c) Alert on kafka.consumer rebalance-rate-per-hour > 5 — fires 10–15 min before lag becomes critical. (d) Add SendGrid latency to your dependency health dashboard; treat >2s P99 as a synthetic alert requiring consumer circuit-breaker activation.

items.drill: Message Queue Backlog Explosion
items.scenario: Consumer lag on a Kafka topic jumped from 0 to 2M messages. Consumers are alive but processing at 1/10th normal speed. A downstream API they call is throttling them.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Set up Kafka + consumer, add latency to downstream call, watch lag grow
2. Diagnose: Check consumer group lag, consumer throughput, downstream API response times
3. Hotfix: Increase consumer instances, add async processing with local buffer, bypass throttled API for non-critical messages
4. Validate: Lag starts decreasing, consumer throughput restored
5. Design: Add backpressure mechanism, dead-letter queue for repeatedly failing messages

items.drill: Split-Brain in Distributed Cache
items.scenario: Network partition splits your 6-node Redis cluster. Both sides elect a master. Writes go to both sides. When partition heals, data conflicts — some users see stale data, some see correct data.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Set up Redis Sentinel, use iptables to simulate network partition
2. Diagnose: Check cluster info, identify which nodes became master, find conflicting keys
3. Hotfix: Force cluster to single master, rebuild replicas from authoritative source
4. Validate: All reads return consistent data, cluster topology is correct
5. Design: Configure min-replicas-to-write, add cluster health monitoring, consider CP vs AP tradeoffs

items.drill: Retry Storm / Amplification
items.scenario: Service A retries 3x to B, B retries 3x to C. C is slow. One user request generates 9 calls to C. Multiply by 1K concurrent users = 9K calls overwhelming C, making it slower, causing more retries.
items.exercise:
1. Reproduce: Chain 3 services with retry logic, inject latency in C, observe exponential amplification
2. Diagnose: Trace request fan-out, count actual calls to C per user request
3. Hotfix: Add retry budget (max 10% of requests can be retries), circuit breaker on B→C
4. Validate: C load drops to manageable level, successful requests still complete
5. Design: Implement hedged requests instead of retries, add jittered exponential backoff

items.drill: Nomad Allocation Restart Loop — Consul Health Check Grace Period Misconfiguration
items.scenario:
At 14:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'payment-service-prod availability 0.0% (threshold: 99.5%)'. Your Go payment service (12 allocations, HashiCorp Nomad 1.7.4, 6 AWS EC2 c6i.4xlarge nodes, us-east-1) processes $2.8M/hour. A rolling deploy of v2.4.7 started at 14:10 UTC. Nomad logs flood with:

  [WARN] nomad: alloc payment-service.[a3f8d] unhealthy after 30s — check 'payment-service-health' critical
  [INFO] nomad: rescheduling alloc payment-service.[a3f8d] due to failed consul health check
  [INFO] nomad: alloc payment-service.[b7c2e] pending→running on i-0a4f9d21
  [WARN] nomad: alloc payment-service.[b7c2e] unhealthy after 30s — check 'payment-service-health' critical

All 12 allocations cycle in perpetual 35-second restart loops. consul health service payment-service shows 12/12 CRITICAL. Zero allocations serving traffic.

Root cause: v2.4.7 introduced pgx v5 lazy connection pooling (lazy=true, min_conns=5, max_conns=25). The Go binary starts and binds :8080 in ~200ms, but GET /health returns HTTP 503 for 14–18s while the pool warms up its 5 idle connections to RDS Multi-AZ (us-east-1, ~8ms connect latency × pool handshake overhead). The Consul service check (interval=5s, timeout=3s, deregister_critical_service_after=30s) fires 3 failed checks within 15s, marks the service CRITICAL, and triggers Nomad to reschedule the allocation. The replacement allocation starts the same 18s warm-up — infinite livelock. Rolling deploy had max_parallel=3, min_healthy_time=5s: all 3 draining instances were stopped before any stabilized, cascading until all 12 were thrashing. Business impact: $65K in failed transactions in 24 minutes; checkout_success_rate 0.0% for 18 minutes.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Run `nomad job status payment-service` — all 12 allocations show 'failed' with reason 'Failed Consul checks'. Run `consul health service payment-service | jq '.[] | {Node:.Node.Node, Status:.Checks[0].Status, Output:.Checks[0].Output}'` — confirms HTTP 503 on /health during first 18s. Check Nomad allocation stderr: `nomad alloc logs -stderr <alloc-id>` — no application errors, normal startup. The service itself is healthy; the check timing is the bug.
2. Immediate mitigation: Stop the restart storm: `nomad job stop -purge payment-service`. Deploy v2.4.6 (last stable): `nomad job run payment-service-v2.4.6.nomad`. Traffic restores in ~45s. Open maintenance window in payment gateway to prevent double-charges from the retry queue.
3. Root cause: Nomad job HCL service block has no check grace period: `check { type = "http", path = "/health", interval = "5s", timeout = "3s", deregister_critical_service_after = "30s" }` — no check_restart grace. Fix: add `check_restart { limit = 5, grace = "20s", ignore_warnings = false }` to the service block, telling Nomad to ignore check failures for the first 20s post-start. Increase `min_healthy_time = "30s"` in the update stanza to prevent rolling before stabilization.
4. Deploy fix: Update job HCL with grace period and min_healthy_time. Re-deploy v2.4.7 with max_parallel=1. Monitor: `watch -n2 'consul health service payment-service | jq ".[0].Checks[0].Status"'` — transitions CRITICAL→PASSING within 25s per allocation.
5. Validate: All 12 allocations reach 'running' + 'healthy'. Consul shows 12/12 PASSING. checkout_success_rate returns to 99.8% within 3 minutes.
6. Prevent: (a) Split /health into /health/ready (returns 200 only after pgxpool.Stat().TotalConns() >= min_conns) and /health/live (returns 200 at process bind). Use /health/ready for Consul check, /health/live for Nomad liveness. (b) CI integration test: start service against a test DB, assert /health/ready returns 200 within 20s. (c) Add check_restart grace_period to the org-wide Nomad job template. (d) PagerDuty alert: `nomad_client_allocs_failed > 3 for same job within 5 minutes` → P1 'allocation restart storm'.

items.drill: Kafka Preferred Leader Election Drift After Broker Restart
items.scenario: At 22:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'checkout-events consumer lag > 480K messages (threshold: 50K)'. Your checkout-events Kafka topic has 24 partitions on AWS MSK (3 brokers, kafka.m5.4xlarge, us-east-1). Grafana shows lag spiking on partitions 0–7 only; partitions 8–23 are nominal at ~200 messages. Broker b-2.checkout-kafka.us-east-1.amazonaws.com restarted at 22:09 UTC after disk I/O saturation (CloudWatch VolumeWriteOps write latency: 4.2s). After broker 2 recovered, preferred leader re-election did not trigger automatically. All 8 partitions originally owned by broker 2 are now led by broker 1. Broker 1 now handles 16 of 24 partitions: CPU at 89%, NetworkOut at 1.78 Gbps (near 2 Gbps ENI limit). Consumer group checkout-consumer-prod (12 Java instances, Confluent Kafka Client 7.4.0) shows fetch-latency-avg 2.1s vs normal 80ms. Broker 1 logs: 'Exhausted available fetch sessions, creating a new one; numPartitions=16' repeating 340 times/min.
items.exercise:
1. Confirm imbalance: Run kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic checkout-events | grep Leader — count partitions per broker. Verify broker 1 leads 16/24 partitions vs expected 8/24.
2. Identify root cause: Check auto.leader.rebalance.enable and leader.imbalance.check.interval.seconds (default 300). Inspect controller logs: grep 'Preferred leader not elected' /var/log/kafka/controller.log. Determine why automatic rebalance did not fire after broker 2 recovered.
3. Hotfix: Trigger manual preferred leader election — kafka-leader-election.sh --bootstrap-server b-1.checkout-kafka.us-east-1.amazonaws.com:9092 --election-type PREFERRED --all-topic-partitions. This is zero-downtime; consumers transparently follow new leaders without offset reset.
4. Validate: Re-run kafka-topics.sh --describe, confirm 8 partitions per broker. Watch Grafana: broker 1 CPU drops 89%→~45% within 2 min, consumer fetch-latency-avg returns to <100ms, lag drains at normal rate (~40K msgs/min).
5. Prevent: Confirm auto.leader.rebalance.enable=true in MSK cluster configuration. Add CloudWatch alarm on custom metric partition-leader-skew > 1.5x. Add 'trigger preferred leader election' step to broker-restart runbook and validate rebalance completed before closing incident.

items.drill: gRPC Context Leak — Goroutine Accumulation from Unbound Background Workers
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Go / gRPC / AWS EKS / Redis ElastiCache)
items.scenario:
At 16:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'ranking-service error_rate 8.7% (threshold: 1%)'. Your Go 1.22 gRPC ranking service (8 pods, AWS EKS us-west-2, c6i.4xlarge, 4GB memory limit) handles 4,200 RPS from the recommendation API. Clients set a 500ms deadline per call. CloudWatch Container Insights shows go_goroutines growing from baseline 1,100 → 22,400 over 47 minutes. Pod memory: 1.2GB → 3.9GB (approaching 4GB limit). Application logs:

  2026-04-23T16:13:02Z WARN  redis: dial tcp 10.0.14.23:6379: connect: connection refused (retrying in 500ms)
  2026-04-23T16:18:44Z ERROR ranking: rpc error: code = DeadlineExceeded desc = context deadline exceeded
  2026-04-23T16:23:07Z ERROR ranking: goroutine_count=18442 exceeds threshold 10000 — OOM risk
  2026-04-23T16:25:13Z ERROR ranking: rpc error: code = ResourceExhausted desc = grpc: server has too many goroutines

A pprof goroutine dump (`kubectl exec -it ranking-service-7d4b9 -- curl -s localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=1`) shows 21,200 goroutines blocked at:

  goroutine 18847 [select, 4 minutes]:
  ranking/internal/scorer.(*Scorer).fetchFeatures(0xc004a82000, {0x0, 0x0}, ...)
    /app/internal/scorer/scorer.go:134 +0x1d8

Root cause: In scorer.go line 134, each gRPC handler spawns `go s.fetchFeatures(context.Background(), userID, features, done)` passing context.Background() instead of the request context. At 16:12:03 UTC, ElastiCache Redis (cluster mode, 3 shards, r7g.2xlarge) underwent a primary shard failover (28s total, confirmed in ElastiCache Events console). Redis client socket read timeout is 5s. With context.Background() goroutines have no deadline — they block on Redis for the full 5s. At 4,200 RPS during the failover: 4,200 req/s × 5s socket timeout = ~21,000 concurrently blocked goroutines, each holding a goroutine stack (8KB min) plus Redis connection object. Total: ~1.8GB of leaked goroutine memory. Client-visible errors came from gRPC's server-side goroutine limit being breached and pods approaching OOM restart.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm goroutine leak is the root cause — `kubectl exec ranking-service-7d4b9 -- curl -s localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=1 | grep -A 3 'select'`. Count goroutines blocking in fetchFeatures vs expected baseline. Cross-reference start time of goroutine growth with ElastiCache Events in AWS Console — look for 'Replication group failover started' event at ~16:12 UTC. Rule out memory leak from heap (check pprof/heap) — confirms goroutine stacks, not heap objects, are consuming memory.
2. Immediate mitigation: Reduce new goroutine creation by rate-limiting RPC acceptance — patch the Envoy sidecar (or Istio VirtualService) to cap ranking-service concurrency at 1,000 in-flight requests (via `http2.initial_stream_window_size` or circuit breaker `max_requests`). This slows new goroutine spawning while the 5s-timeout backlog drains naturally. Alternatively trigger a rolling restart (`kubectl rollout restart deployment/ranking-service`) — each pod drains goroutines on SIGTERM, restarting clean. Ensure Redis has fully failed over before restart to avoid re-triggering.
3. Root cause: In scorer.go locate: `go s.fetchFeatures(context.Background(), ...)`. The fix: replace `context.Background()` with the request context `ctx` passed into the gRPC handler. This ties goroutine lifetime to the client deadline — when the 500ms client deadline fires, the Redis call is cancelled via `context.WithDeadline` propagation, goroutine exits immediately. `grep -rn 'context.Background()' internal/scorer/` to find all instances.
4. Fix: Change `go s.fetchFeatures(context.Background(), userID, featuresCh, done)` → `go s.fetchFeatures(ctx, userID, featuresCh, done)`. For any goroutines that must legitimately outlive a request (e.g., async prefetch warming), create a bounded context: `prefetchCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 1500*time.Millisecond); defer cancel(); go s.prefetchNextPage(prefetchCtx, ...)`. Add a goroutine leak test: `import go.uber.org/goleak; defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)` to all scorer handler unit tests — this fails CI if any goroutine is left running after the test.
5. Redis resilience: Add a gobreaker circuit breaker on the Redis client — `cb := gobreaker.NewCircuitBreaker(gobreaker.Settings{Name: "redis", MaxRequests: 5, Interval: 10*time.Second, Timeout: 1*time.Second, ReadyToTrip: func(c gobreaker.Counts) bool { return c.ConsecutiveFailures > 3 }})`. When Redis is in failover, breaker opens after 3 consecutive failures, fetchFeatures returns cache-miss (fallback to DynamoDB cold path) in <1ms. Goroutine lifetime drops from 5s → <1ms during outage, eliminating the accumulation entirely.
6. Validate: After deploying the context propagation fix, confirm goroutine count returns to ~1,200 baseline within 5 min. pprof goroutine dump should show 0 goroutines blocked in fetchFeatures. Load test with `ghz -c 200 -n 100000 --proto ranking.proto ranking-service:50051 /RankingService.ScoreItems` — inject Redis latency (tc netem delay 3000ms on the Redis endpoint) and confirm goroutine count stays flat.
7. Prevent: (a) Add a Prometheus gauge: `promauto.NewGaugeFunc(prometheus.GaugeOpts{Name: "go_goroutines"}, func() float64 { return float64(runtime.NumGoroutine()) })` — alert when `go_goroutines > 5000` for 2 min. (b) Enforce context propagation via `staticcheck SA1012` linting rule in CI — flags `context.Background()` inside gRPC unary/streaming handler functions. (c) Add goleak to all gRPC handler integration tests. (d) Set Redis client `ReadTimeout: 800*time.Millisecond` (≤ gRPC client deadline) so socket timeout is bounded even without context cancellation.

items.drill: Apache Flink RocksDB Checkpoint Timeout — L0 Compaction I/O Starvation on Feature Pipeline
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Apache Flink 1.18 / GKE 1.29 / Java / RocksDB / GCS)
items.scenario:
At 08:42 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'feature-pipeline-prod flink_checkpoint_duration_seconds 197 (threshold: 150, timeout: 180s)'. Your Apache Flink 1.18.1 streaming job (32 TaskManagers × 8 slots on GKE 1.29, PD-SSD storage for RocksDB state backend) computes real-time user features for 14 ML ranking models, processing 2.8M events/sec from Google Cloud Pub/Sub. RocksDB state: 280GB across 32 TMs (user_activity_state KeyedStream, ~8.7M distinct keys, avg entry 32KB). Checkpoint interval: 5 min. Checkpoint storage: GCS gs://flink-checkpoints-prod. Checkpoint timeout: 3 min.

Flink logs (Cloud Logging):

  2026-05-07T08:39:14Z INFO  CheckpointCoordinator - Triggering checkpoint 8,447 @ 1746607154000
  2026-05-07T08:40:51Z WARN  CheckpointCoordinator - Checkpoint 8,447 expired before completing (97.4s elapsed, 12/32 TaskManagers acknowledged)
  2026-05-07T08:42:00Z WARN  CheckpointCoordinator - 3 consecutive checkpoint failures. Last successful: 8,446 at 08:34:14 UTC (8 min ago)
  2026-05-07T08:42:11Z ERROR TaskManager [tm-17] - RocksDB async snapshot timed out after 174000ms
  2026-05-07T08:42:11Z INFO  RocksDBKeyedStateBackend - Column family user_activity_state: L0 files=44 (stall trigger=4). SST flush stalled.

Cloud Monitoring — all 32 TM nodes:
  disk/write_bytes_count: 1.78 GB/s  (PD-SSD sustained write max: 1.8 GB/s — 99% saturated)
  disk/read_bytes_count:  0.91 GB/s  (checkpoint snapshot reads blocked behind compaction writes)
  cpu/utilization: 87%

Root cause: At 07:15 UTC, the upstream Pub/Sub publisher increased event batch size 128KB→512KB, quadrupling per-second RocksDB write volume. All 32 TMs simultaneously triggered L0→L1 compaction (L0 files: 44 vs trigger: 4). Concurrent compaction across 32 TMs saturated PD-SSD write bandwidth. Flink's async snapshot mechanism cannot read RocksDB state pages during an active L0 write stall; snapshot threads block until the stall clears, then time out at 174s. On the 4th consecutive checkpoint failure at 08:47 UTC, Flink restarts the job from checkpoint 8,446 (08:34 UTC). Full restart takes 14 minutes (280GB state restore from GCS at ~330 MB/s). During restart, all 14 ranking models serve 22-minute-old stale features from the feature store. Business impact: CTR −12%, conversion −4.2%, estimated $340K revenue impact, SLA breach for 4 B2B accounts on guaranteed feature freshness SLAs (<5 min staleness).
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Check Flink REST API — GET /jobs/{job-id}/checkpoints — confirm 3+ consecutive failures and last successful checkpoint timestamp. Check Cloud Monitoring Disk Write Bytes across all TM nodes: confirm ≥1.78 GB/s (near PD-SSD 1.8 GB/s limit). Verify RocksDB L0 stall via Flink Prometheus metrics: flink_taskmanager_job_task_operator_rocksdb_num_files_at_level0 >= 4 (write stall trigger) and flink_taskmanager_job_task_operator_rocksdb_actual_delayed_write_rate > 0. Rule out JobManager GC and Pub/Sub consumer lag as root cause (check flink_jobmanager_Status_JVM_GC_G1_Old_Generation_Time and pubsub.googleapis.com/subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age).
2. Immediate mitigation: Extend checkpoint timeout to 10 minutes — update flink-conf.yaml ConfigMap: state.checkpoints.timeout=600000, then kubectl rollout restart deployment/flink-jobmanager. This prevents Flink from aborting in-progress checkpoints during compaction. Simultaneously throttle RocksDB compaction thread count: set state.backend.rocksdb.thread.num=2 (down from default 8) in the ConfigMap and rolling-restart TMs in batches of 4 (leaving 28 active to sustain processing). Compaction I/O drops from 1.78 GB/s to ~0.45 GB/s, freeing disk bandwidth for snapshot reads. Monitor flink_taskmanager_job_task_operator_rocksdb_num_files_at_level0 across TMs — expect L0 file count to drain below 4 within 20 min.
3. Identify upstream cause: Query Cloud Trace: filter jsonPayload.batch_size_bytes > 200000 AND startTime > '2026-05-07T07:00:00Z'. Confirm batch size jumped to 512KB at 07:15 UTC. Contact the Pub/Sub publisher team to revert to 128KB batch size or apply flow control (PubSub publisher FlowControlSettings: maxOutstandingBytes=128MB). The 4× write reduction allows L0 compaction to complete before the next trigger cycle within 10–15 min.
4. Validate: After compaction throttle is applied and batch size reverts, monitor flink_taskmanager_job_task_operator_rocksdb_num_files_at_level0 — should drop from 44 to < 4. Checkpoint duration (flink_jobmanager_job_lastCheckpointDuration) should fall below 90s. Confirm checkpoint 8,448+ completes successfully via Flink Web UI → Checkpoints → Completed. Feature store write timestamp lag should return to < 5 min (internal SLA dashboard). Verify B2B SLA compliance is restored.
5. Prevent: (a) Migrate TM node pool to GKE nodes with local NVMe SSD (n2-standard-32 + local-ssd ephemeral storage, ~2.4M IOPS vs PD-SSD 25K IOPS) — eliminates shared network disk as the I/O ceiling. (b) Enable RocksDB managed memory: state.backend.rocksdb.memory.managed=true — caps total memtable memory, prevents simultaneous L0 flush avalanches. (c) Add early checkpoint degradation alert: flink_jobmanager_job_lastCheckpointDuration > 120s (0.67× timeout) → PagerDuty P2 'checkpoint degraded', giving 60s lead time to act before job restart. (d) Add Pub/Sub publisher contract validation in CI: integration test asserts average batch_size_bytes ≤ 131072 before publisher deploy passes. (e) Add RocksDB L0 stall alert: flink_taskmanager_job_task_operator_rocksdb_num_files_at_level0 > 10 for 5 minutes → PagerDuty P2 'RocksDB compaction pressure'.

items.drill: AWS Kinesis ShardIterator Expiry — Silent Audit Gap from LATEST Recovery Bug After Maintenance Pause
items.layer: Distributed Systems (AWS Kinesis Data Streams / Python / boto3)
items.scenario:
At 03:52 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'inference-audit-compliance: zero S3 audit records for 02:58–03:52 UTC (expected ~2,400/min; found 0 in Athena query)'. Your Python Kinesis consumer (kinesis-audit-consumer, EC2 c6i.2xlarge, us-east-1) reads ML inference results from stream inference-results-prod (4 shards, 7-day retention, ~2,400 records/min) and writes structured records to s3://audit-logs-prod/inference/ for SOC 2 Type II compliance.

At 03:00 UTC, on-call engineer SIGSTOPped the consumer process for a planned SageMaker endpoint maintenance window and SIGCONTd it at 03:05:22 UTC. The shard iterator fetched at consumer bootstrap (02:14 UTC) expired at 03:05:47 UTC — exactly 25 seconds after process resume (Kinesis shard iterators expire after exactly 300s of inactivity). The first get_records call after resume:

  botocore.errorfactory.ExpiredIteratorException: The iterator is expired.
  Please use GetShardIterator to obtain a new iterator.
  Status Code: 400, Error Code: ExpiredIteratorException,
  Request ID: a8f4c21e-7d93-4b1e-8203-9b2c4d5e6f1a

The error recovery handler contained a critical bug — recovery used ShardIteratorType=LATEST instead of the last checkpointed sequence number:

  except kinesis_client.exceptions.ExpiredIteratorException:
      logger.warning('Shard iterator expired, refreshing...')
      resp = kinesis_client.get_shard_iterator(
          StreamName='inference-results-prod',
          ShardId=shard_id,
          ShardIteratorType='LATEST'  # BUG: silently skips all records since last checkpoint
      )
      shard_iterator = resp['ShardIterator']

LATEST repositions the iterator at the current stream tip (03:05:47 UTC), silently discarding all records produced between the last successful S3 checkpoint (02:58:33 UTC) and 03:05:47 UTC — a 7-minute-14-second gap containing 17,280 records across 4 shards.

CloudWatch metrics appear completely healthy: GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds drops to ~1,200ms (normal real-time lag). GetRecords.Success = 100%. No alarm fires. The consumer processes current records normally, unaware of the gap. Compliance team discovers the missing data 47 minutes later via an Athena query on the S3 audit bucket.

Business impact: SOC 2 Type II audit violation — data processing agreement mandates a complete audit trail for all ML model decisions. Customer breach notification required. $180K compliance remediation and audit re-certification cost. Emergency 3-hour incident to reconstruct records from Kinesis 7-day retention.
items.exercise:
1. Confirm gap exists in Kinesis (data is NOT lost — only skipped): aws kinesis get-shard-iterator --stream-name inference-results-prod --shard-id shardId-000000000000 --shard-iterator-type AT_TIMESTAMP --timestamp '2026-05-08T02:58:00Z' — iterate with get-records to confirm records exist for the missing window in Kinesis. Cross-reference consumer stderr log for 'Shard iterator expired, refreshing...' at 03:05:47 UTC. Check EC2 system log for SIGSTOP/SIGCONT events at 03:00 UTC and 03:05 UTC. This confirms: records are in Kinesis (7-day retention intact); only the LATEST recovery bug caused the consumer to skip them.
2. Immediate gap recovery: Write a one-off replay script for all 4 shards — get AT_TIMESTAMP iterator starting at '2026-05-08T02:58:33Z' (last checkpoint), call get_records in a loop, write each record to s3://audit-logs-prod/inference/ preserving the original ApproximateArrivalTimestamp, stop when ApproximateArrivalTimestamp > '2026-05-08T03:05:47Z'. Validate: Athena query on the S3 bucket for the gap window now returns ~17,280 records. Cross-check per-shard record counts via aws kinesis get-records to ensure no shard was missed.
3. Root cause fix: Replace ShardIteratorType=LATEST in the ExpiredIteratorException handler with AFTER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER using the DynamoDB checkpoint: last_seq = checkpoint_table.get_item(Key={'stream_shard': f'inference-results-prod#{shard_id}'})['Item']['sequence_number']; resp = kinesis_client.get_shard_iterator(StreamName='inference-results-prod', ShardId=shard_id, ShardIteratorType='AFTER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER', StartingSequenceNumber=last_seq). Add a unit test with a mock that injects ExpiredIteratorException and asserts the recovery call uses AFTER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER — fails CI on any future regression.
4. Durable per-shard checkpointing: Write the last committed sequence number to DynamoDB after every successful S3 flush — checkpoint_table.put_item(Item={'stream_shard': f'inference-results-prod#{shard_id}', 'sequence_number': last_seq_number, 'updated_at': datetime.utcnow().isoformat()}). On any restart or iterator recovery, always seed shard iterators from DynamoDB checkpoints rather than LATEST or TRIM_HORIZON.
5. Add gap-detection monitoring: Emit a custom CloudWatch metric inference_audit_records_written (count) every 60s from within the consumer. Alarm: Sum(inference_audit_records_written, 5min) < 10000 (expected ~12,000) → PagerDuty P1 'audit pipeline gap'. This fires within 5 minutes of any gap beginning — vs. the 47-minute discovery delay in this incident.
6. Prevent: (a) Migrate to Kinesis Client Library (KCL 2.x) or AWS Lambda event source mapping — both manage shard iterator lifecycle, checkpointing, and re-sharding automatically, eliminating this class of bug entirely. (b) Add pre-maintenance runbook step: 'Gracefully shut down the consumer (SIGTERM) and wait for the process to exit and checkpoint, then begin maintenance. Never SIGSTOP a stateful consumer for more than 60 seconds.' (c) Add CloudWatch alarm on GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds p99 > 300000ms (5-min lag) — catches consumer stalls before data loss occurs. (d) Add LocalStack integration test: publish 1000 records, SIGSTOP/SIGCONT consumer after 290s, assert all 1000 records appear in S3 (verifying correct iterator recovery type).

items.drill: Tokio Ack Handler Shutdown Race — Pub/Sub Redelivery Storm During GKE Rolling Update
items.layer: Distributed Systems (GCP Pub/Sub / Rust / Tokio 1.35 / GKE 1.29)
items.scenario:
At 11:23 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'pubsub-consumer-prod pubsub_subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age > 600s (threshold: 60s)'. Your Rust Pub/Sub consumer (order-events-consumer v2.4.3, 8 pods on GKE 1.29, n2-standard-8, us-central1) processes the order-events subscription (~14,000 messages/min, ack_deadline=60s). Cloud Monitoring: pubsub/subscription/undelivered_message_count climbs from baseline 2K → 340K in 12 minutes. Subscriber ack rate: ~0 messages/min. All ack_deadline_exceeded messages redeliver every 60 seconds.

Cloud Logging on the first terminating pod (11:11:03 UTC):

  2026-05-13T11:11:03.447Z ERROR order_events_consumer::ack_handler - JoinSet task dropped before ack completion: projects/prod-ml/subscriptions/order-events ackId='AEHentgc...' seq=order-events#shard0#8823441
  2026-05-13T11:11:03.449Z WARN  order_events_consumer::runtime - Tokio shutdown_timeout(30s) reached; dropping 847 in-flight ack futures

Root cause: GKE rolling update (maxUnavailable=1, maxSurge=1) began at 11:11 UTC, sending SIGTERM to pod 0. The Rust binary calls `runtime.shutdown_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))`. The ack path spawns fire-and-forget tasks with `tokio::spawn(ack_batch(client.clone(), ack_ids))` — detached from the parent subscription loop. Tokio's `shutdown_timeout` cancels all incomplete spawned tasks after 30s regardless of in-flight gRPC state. The google-cloud-pubsub 0.24.0 ack gRPC call is mid-flight when the task is dropped: no acknowledgment reaches the Pub/Sub service. After 60s the messages redeliver to the next available pod — which is also mid-shutdown (8-pod rolling update spans 12 minutes). Circular redelivery: 847 messages per pod × 8 pods = 6,776 messages never committed. Each 60s redeliver cycle adds to undelivered_message_count. By 11:23 UTC, 340K messages have accumulated across redelivery rounds.

Downstream impact: 28 order-processing services receive duplicate event triggers. Idempotency keys on most paths prevent double-charges, but 3,100 customers receive duplicate confirmation emails and 22 receive duplicate discount redemptions ($190K gross exposure, $41K actual duplicate discounts applied before reconciliation catches it). Three B2B SLA customers breach their guaranteed processing latency SLA (15s P99 → 74s P99 during the storm).
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm ack failure is consumer-side (not Pub/Sub system error): Cloud Monitoring → pubsub/subscription/ack_message_count should show drop to 0 at 11:11 UTC correlating with rolling update start. Run: kubectl rollout history deployment/order-events-consumer -n prod — confirm revision 24 rollout started at 11:11:00 UTC. Check Cloud Logging (filter: resource.type=k8s_container, textPayload:'ack_handler'): count 'task dropped before ack completion' log lines per pod. Verify duplicate processing downstream: SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) FROM order_events_log WHERE event_time BETWEEN '2026-05-13T11:11:00Z' AND '2026-05-13T11:23:00Z' GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 — confirms specific order IDs received 2+ deliveries.
2. Immediate mitigation: Pause the rolling update to stop new pod terminations: kubectl rollout pause deployment/order-events-consumer -n prod. Scale to 0 replicas and back to 8 to flush all in-flight redelivered messages through a clean consumer: kubectl scale deployment order-events-consumer -n prod --replicas=0 && sleep 10 && kubectl scale deployment order-events-consumer -n prod --replicas=8. This breaks the redelivery cycle. Trigger downstream deduplication: publish order IDs from the 11:11–11:23 window to a dedup-check-topic; downstream services replay with idempotency checks and suppress duplicates.
3. Root cause — fire-and-forget tokio::spawn breaks structured shutdown: In src/ack_handler.rs locate the ack path: tokio::spawn(ack_batch(client.clone(), ack_ids)) — a detached task with no reference held by the parent. Fix: Replace with a shared JoinSet tracked in the subscription loop struct: add field ack_tasks: JoinSet<Result<(), AckError>> to the SubscriptionWorker. Spawn acks via self.ack_tasks.spawn(ack_batch(client.clone(), ack_ids)). In the SIGTERM handler, before returning from main: while let Some(res) = ack_tasks.join_next().await { res??; } — drains all in-flight acks before allowing shutdown to complete.
4. SIGTERM handler with drain guard: Implement explicit graceful drain using tokio::signal: (a) on SIGTERM, set an AtomicBool stop_pulling = true — subscription loop stops calling subscriber.pull(); (b) wait for all in-flight message handlers to complete via a tokio::sync::Barrier or JoinSet drain; (c) flush all pending acks synchronously; (d) only then return from main (allowing the Tokio runtime to drop). Set Kubernetes terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 90 — exceeds the 60s Pub/Sub ack deadline to guarantee acks complete before SIGKILL.
5. Validate: Deploy the fix (the paused rollout: kubectl rollout resume deployment/order-events-consumer). Monitor during rolling update: Cloud Monitoring pubsub/subscription/ack_message_count should remain flat at ~14,000/min with no drop per pod termination. pubsub/subscription/undelivered_message_count should stay below 5K throughout. Cloud Logging: zero 'task dropped before ack' log lines during subsequent rollouts. Run duplicate check query again post-rollout — expect zero duplicate order IDs.
6. Prevent: (a) Add a Tokio graceful-shutdown integration test using the Pub/Sub emulator (pubsub-emulator Docker image): publish 500 messages, send SIGTERM to the consumer process via nix::sys::signal::kill() mid-processing, assert all 500 ackIds appear in the emulator's acknowledged list — this test would have caught the bug in CI. (b) Add a Clippy lint (custom lint via dylint) that flags bare tokio::spawn() calls inside functions named *handler* or *subscriber* — enforces JoinSet usage for tracked async tasks in consumer code. (c) Add Cloud Monitoring alert: rate(pubsub_subscription_ack_message_count[2m]) < 5000 AND rate(pubsub_subscription_pull_message_count[2m]) > 5000 → PagerDuty P1 'consumer ack stall'. This fires within 2 minutes of the next redelivery storm vs the 12-minute gap in this incident. (d) Document the structured-concurrency rule in CONTRIBUTING.md: 'All async tasks that perform external side effects (acks, writes, RPC calls) MUST be tracked in a JoinSet or equivalent — no bare tokio::spawn() for work that must complete before shutdown.'

items.drill: Elasticsearch Disk Flood Stage — force_merge Segment Merge Overhead Triggers Mass Index Read-Only Lockout
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Elasticsearch 8.12.2 / AWS ECS / Java Spring Boot)
items.scenario:
At 07:33 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'event-ingestion-service-prod error_rate 100% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Elasticsearch 8.12.2 cluster (6 data nodes, AWS ECS, r6g.4xlarge, 500GB gp3 EBS each, us-east-1) stores 2.1TB of event analytics data across 48 daily indices (event-logs-YYYY.MM.DD, 3 primary shards × 1 replica each). Disk per-node at baseline: 71% (355GB/500GB).

At 07:15 UTC, an automated daily maintenance job force-merges 6 aging indices (30 days old) to reclaim segment file handles:

  POST /event-logs-2026.04.*/_forcemerge?max_num_segments=1

A force_merge with max_num_segments=1 temporarily writes the new merged segment alongside the existing segments before deleting the originals. The 6 indices being merged hold 280GB. Merge overhead adds ~40% of the source size in temporary disk:
  355GB (baseline) + 112GB (merge temporaries) = 467GB / 500GB = 93.4% — approaching flood stage.

At 07:26:33 UTC, JVM heap pressure on data-node-4 (28GB G1 heap) triggers back-to-back Full GC pauses, deferring segment file descriptor release for 4.2s:

  [2026-05-20T07:26:33.847+0000][gc] GC(9284) Pause Full (G1 Evacuation Pause) 25188M->23941M(28672M) 4.201s
  [2026-05-20T07:26:42.019+0000][gc] GC(9285) Pause Full (System.gc()) 26001M->24104M(28672M) 3.971s

The GC-deferred release spikes node-4 disk to 95.7%, crossing Elasticsearch's cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage (default 95%). Elasticsearch logs at 07:26:44 UTC:

  WARN  [o.e.c.r.a.DiskThresholdMonitor] [data-node-4] flood stage disk watermark [95%] exceeded on [node-4][/data/nodes/0] free: 17.8gb[3.6%], all indices on this node will be marked read-only
  INFO  [o.e.c.m.MetadataIndexStateService] [master-1] adding block BLOCK_WRITES to index [event-logs-2026.05.20], cause [flood stage disk watermark exceeded on node data-node-4]
  [47 additional identical lines — one per index cluster-wide]

ES 8.x flood-stage behavior: when ANY node crosses flood stage, Elasticsearch applies index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete=true to ALL indices cluster-wide (not just those on the affected node). All writes fail immediately. Spring Boot application logs:

  2026-05-20T07:33:02.441Z ERROR ElasticsearchBulkProcessor - BulkItemResponse failure:
    ClusterBlockException[blocked by: [TOO_MANY_REQUESTS/12/disk usage exceeded flood-stage watermark, index has read-only-allow-delete block];]
    index=event-logs-2026.05.20 shard=2 documentCount=500 bulkSizeBytes=14882
    [847 requests/sec, all returning HTTP 403]
  2026-05-20T07:33:03.891Z WARN  RetryHandler - Retrying bulk request attempt 2/3 (max retries: 3, drop after)

The force_merge completes at 07:28:51 UTC. Old segments deleted. Disk drops to 73.2% — well below flood stage. But Elasticsearch does NOT automatically remove read_only_allow_delete blocks after disk recovers. The block persists until explicitly cleared via the settings API.

Grafana Elasticsearch cluster health dashboard shows: cluster_status=GREEN, unassigned_shards=0, disk_used_percent=73.2%. The write block is invisible to standard ES health metrics. On-call spends 18 minutes investigating the Spring Boot application layer before checking index blocks. During that time, 304,920 events (847/sec × 360s) are silently dropped after 3 retries with no dead-letter recovery path. Three downstream ML models serving personalization features read stale data for 14 minutes post-recovery. Business impact: 304,920 lost behavioral events, 3 ML feature pipelines degraded, estimated $110K revenue from degraded personalization CTR during the blackout window.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — check index blocks directly (not cluster health): GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete — returns all indices with block set to true. If this returns a large number of indices, it confirms flood-stage lockout. Standard health endpoints (GET /_cluster/health, Kibana index management) will show GREEN/healthy — the write block is invisible to them. Cross-reference: search CloudWatch Logs for /aws/ecs/elasticsearch-cluster with filter 'flood stage disk watermark exceeded' — confirms the event at 07:26:44 UTC on data-node-4. Check current disk usage: GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,disk.used_percent,disk.avail — should show 73.2% (recovered). This proves the flood stage was transient.
2. Immediate fix — clear the read_only_allow_delete block: PUT /_all/_settings {"index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete": null}. The null value removes the setting entirely. This is zero-downtime — no restart, no shard movement. Verify: GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete should return an empty object (no indices with the block). Application writes resume within 1–2 seconds as the Spring Boot BulkProcessor retry queue drains. Monitor event-ingestion-service error rate in CloudWatch — should return to 0% within 30s.
3. Root cause — force_merge merge overhead + GC interaction: The automated maintenance job ran force_merge at 07:15 on 280GB of indices with max_num_segments=1. The merge generates temporary disk usage equal to 30–50% of source data while old and new segments coexist. On data-node-4, the G1GC Full GC pause (4.2s) deferred OS-level segment file release for ~4s, pushing disk utilization the final 1.7% past flood stage. Run: GET /_tasks?detailed=true&actions=*forcemerge to see active merge tasks; check CloudWatch Logs for GC pause events preceding the flood-stage alert.
4. Recover lost events: 304,920 dropped events have no automatic recovery — the ingestion service discards after 3 retries. Add a dead-letter SQS queue as an emergency DLQ: when ES bulk returns 403, serialize raw events to SQS dead-letter-events-prod for later replay. Immediately check whether events from the 07:26–07:33 window still exist in the upstream Kinesis stream (7-day retention): aws kinesis get-shard-iterator --stream-name event-stream-prod --shard-iterator-type AT_TIMESTAMP --timestamp '2026-05-20T07:26:00Z' — iterate and replay to ES now that the block is cleared.
5. Fix the merge strategy: max_num_segments=1 maximizes merge overhead. Switch to max_num_segments=5 (reduces temporary disk overhead to ~10% of source size). Better: for 30-day-old indices, call DELETE /event-logs-2026.04.* instead of force_merge — this frees disk entirely with zero overhead. If merging is required for performance (read optimization), run it node-by-node with a per-node disk check first: GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,disk.used_percent — abort the merge if any node exceeds 80%. Add to the maintenance job: assert all nodes < 80% disk before calling _forcemerge.
6. Prevent: (a) Automated block remediation — deploy a Lambda that runs every 2 minutes: (1) calls GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete, (2) if any blocks found AND GET /_cat/nodes shows all disk < 85%, automatically calls PUT /_all/_settings {"index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete": null} and pages PagerDuty P2 'ES flood-stage auto-cleared'. This reduces the 18-minute human discovery delay to <2 minutes. (b) Synthetic write health check: CloudWatch Events rule triggers a Lambda every 60s to PUT a test doc to health-check-write-index and assert HTTP 200 (not 403). This catches any write block within 60s regardless of cause — invisible to all standard ES health metrics. (c) Add CloudWatch alarm on custom metric es_disk_used_percent_max > 85% for any node (pushed from a Lambda polling GET /_cat/nodes every minute) → PagerDuty P2 'ES disk high-watermark approaching flood stage'. At 73% baseline this fires with 12% headroom before flood stage — enough time to pause maintenance jobs. (d) Set cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.high: '88%' and cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage: '95%' explicitly in elasticsearch.yml — prevents surprises from default changes across upgrades. Update these values whenever baseline disk usage increases above 75%.

items.drill: SQS Visibility Timeout Race — Lambda Duration Spike Causes Duplicate Payment Charges During Flash Sale
items.layer: Distributed Systems (AWS SQS / Lambda Node.js 20.x / DynamoDB / Stripe API)
items.scenario:
At 19:31 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'payment-service-prod Stripe duplicate_charge_detected 43/min (threshold: 0)'. Your payment processing pipeline (AWS Lambda Node.js 20.x, memory=1,536MB, timeout=60s) is triggered by SQS queue payment-processing-prod (standard queue, VisibilityTimeout=30s, batch size=1). A flash sale at 19:15 UTC drove Lambda ConcurrentExecutions from 5 → 52 within 6 minutes. Lambda Duration baseline is 17s per order (Stripe API: 280ms; SageMaker fraud endpoint ml-fraud-score-prod: 420ms; DynamoDB reads/writes: 180ms; overhead/logging/config: ~16s). During the sale two downstream dependencies degrade simultaneously:
  — Stripe payment API: 280ms → 1.2s average (load-related; Stripe declares no incident)
  — SageMaker fraud endpoint: 420ms → 9.8s P99 (ml-fraud-score-prod's 2-instance concurrency limit saturated by 52 simultaneous Lambda invocations)

Combined, Lambda Duration P99 rises from 17,100ms → 31,240ms, exceeding VisibilityTimeout=30,000ms.

CloudWatch Logs Insights (`filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/`) on /aws/lambda/payment-processor-prod:

  2026-05-19T19:27:14.182Z WARN  PaymentHandler - SQS re-delivery: MessageId=f3a9b1c2-8e7d-4f2e-9b3c-6d4e5f7a8b9c OrderId=ORD-74821 ReceiveCount=2
  [6,218 identical warnings between 19:24–19:51 UTC]
  2026-05-19T19:27:14.331Z INFO  StripeService - charge.create orderId=ORD-74821 chargeId=ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ5 amount=18700 currency=usd status=succeeded (DUPLICATE)
  2026-05-19T19:27:14.898Z ERROR IdempotencyGuard - ConditionalCheckFailedException orderId=ORD-74821 — existing chargeId=ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ4 vs new ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ5. Stripe charge NOT rolled back.

CloudWatch metrics (19:24–19:51 UTC):
  Lambda/Duration P99: 31,240ms (baseline: 17,100ms)
  Lambda/ConcurrentExecutions: 52
  Lambda/Throttles: 0 (no reserved concurrency cap configured)
  SQS VisibilityTimeout (configured): 30s
  DynamoDB/ConditionalCheckFailedRequests on payment_idempotency: 6,218
  Stripe Dashboard: 847 unique orders confirmed with duplicate charge.succeeded events

Root cause — two compounding bugs:
(1) SQS VisibilityTimeout=30s was set at queue creation in 2024 when Lambda Duration baseline was 8s. As payment logic grew (fraud model, feature enrichment, audit logging), Duration reached 17s — still within 30s until the first multi-dependency degradation event. No alarm monitored Duration relative to VisibilityTimeout.
(2) Idempotency guard fires in the wrong order: the Lambda calls stripe.charges.create() BEFORE writing the DynamoDB idempotency record. A re-delivered Lambda (starting at T=30s) reads DynamoDB at T=30.1s — the original Lambda is mid-execution between its Stripe call (T=25s) and its DynamoDB write (T=26.3s). No idempotency record exists yet → re-delivery passes the check → Stripe is called → duplicate charge committed. When the re-delivered Lambda then tries its own DynamoDB PutItem, the ConditionalCheckFailedException is caught and logged, but the handler returns 200 OK without calling stripe.refunds.create() to reverse the already-committed duplicate charge.

Business impact: 847 customers double-charged ($187 average = $158,389 total). Customer support +1,200 inbound tickets within 20 minutes. PCI-DSS incident notification triggered. Stripe chargeback exposure: $158,389 × 2.1% estimated rate = $3,326.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm SQS re-delivery (not Lambda crash-loop) as root cause. In Logs Insights on /aws/lambda/payment-processor-prod: `fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/ | stats count() by bin(1min)` — confirm 6,218 events between 19:24–19:51 UTC. Verify Duration P99 via CloudWatch Metrics → AWS/Lambda → Duration → p99 = 31,240ms. Confirm VisibilityTimeout: `aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/payment-processing-prod --attribute-names VisibilityTimeout` → '30'. Open X-Ray Service Map (19:15–19:52 UTC): identify ml-fraud-score-prod P99 = 9.8s and Stripe API P99 = 1.2s as the two dependencies driving Duration P99 above the 30s threshold. Confirm the idempotency ordering bug: grep handler code for the Stripe call position relative to the DynamoDB PutItem — Stripe appears on line 47, DynamoDB write on line 68, meaning 1.3s of vulnerability window exists between them where a re-delivery succeeds.
2. Immediate mitigation: (a) Increase VisibilityTimeout to 120s: `aws sqs set-queue-attributes --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/payment-processing-prod --attributes '{"VisibilityTimeout":"120"}'`. All Lambdas complete within 60s (Lambda timeout ceiling); 120s provides a 2× buffer against any downstream degradation. Blocks all new duplicate charges immediately. (b) Throttle Lambda concurrency to relieve SageMaker endpoint pressure: `aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name payment-processor-prod --reserved-concurrent-executions 10`. At 10 concurrent Lambdas × 1 SageMaker call each = 10 concurrent fraud inferences — within the endpoint's provisioned throughput. SageMaker P99 should recover from 9.8s to <1s within 90s. (c) Begin batch-refunding 847 duplicates: `stripe charges list --created[gte]=1747683900 --created[lte]=1747686660 | jq '.data[] | select(.metadata.is_redelivery=="true") | .id'` → `stripe refunds create --charge=<id> --reason=duplicate`. Log all refund IDs in the P1 incident ticket for compliance.
3. Fix idempotency order (root-cause fix): In src/handlers/payment.js, restructure processing: (a) FIRST: DynamoDB PutItem with ConditionExpression='attribute_not_exists(orderId)' and TTL attribute set to Math.floor(Date.now()/1000)+3600. On ConditionalCheckFailedException → this orderId is already in-flight or completed; return HTTP 200 immediately without any Stripe call. (b) ONLY after PutItem succeeds: call `await stripe.charges.create(payload, {idempotencyKey: 'order-'+orderId})`. (c) On Stripe error: DeleteItem the DynamoDB record to allow a clean retry. This structure makes the DynamoDB PutItem a distributed mutex — only one Lambda can win the conditional write and proceed to Stripe regardless of SQS re-delivery timing or concurrency. Deploy via Lambda versioning blue/green: `aws lambda publish-version --function-name payment-processor-prod` → update alias `stable` to new version → SQS Event Source Mapping already points to alias.
4. Add Stripe-native idempotency as defense-in-depth: Pass `idempotencyKey` on every Stripe call: `await stripe.charges.create({amount, currency, source}, {idempotencyKey: 'order-'+orderId+'-v1'})`. Stripe deduplicates charges sharing the same key within 24h and returns the original charge object on retry with no new payment created. This backstop operates independently of Lambda/DynamoDB state and survives any future idempotency regressions.
5. Validate: Deploy fixed handler. Load-test with 100 orders and injected delays (mock Stripe SDK at 1.3s, mock SageMaker SDK at 10s): (a) Logs Insights — `filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/` returns 0 results with VisibilityTimeout=120s. (b) Force-inject a re-delivery scenario in staging: reduce VisibilityTimeout to 5s, process 20 orders with 8s artificial delay — assert DynamoDB ConditionalCheckFailedRequests=20 (one per re-delivery) and Stripe duplicate charge count=0. (c) Production validation: `stripe charges list --created[gte]=<deploy_epoch> | jq '[.data | group_by(.metadata.order_id)[] | select(length > 1)] | length'` must return 0.
6. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm: AWS/Lambda Duration P99 > (SQS VisibilityTimeout × 0.75) for 3 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P2 'payment Lambda P99 approaching SQS VisibilityTimeout — duplicate charge risk'. At VisibilityTimeout=120s this fires when P99 > 90s, leaving 30s headroom before re-queuing begins. Re-evaluate the threshold whenever Lambda code or queue attributes change — add to service runbook. (b) Enforce Lambda scaling cap via Event Source Mapping: `aws lambda update-event-source-mapping --uuid <esm-uuid> --scaling-config '{"MaximumConcurrency": 12}'` — caps Lambda instances at the ESM layer regardless of SQS depth, preventing SageMaker endpoint saturation during demand spikes. (c) Add SQS Dead Letter Queue (maxReceiveCount=3): any message re-delivered 3+ times moves to payment-processing-dlq and pages PagerDuty P2 — ensures no order is silently dropped or processed in an infinite loop.
